date: Reviewed in the United States on January 1, 2023
author: TSW
title: Expensive rice cooker, not worth the hype
body: Thought I was getting a high end rice cooker, however, got a expensive low end rice cooker.  The non-stick cooking pot coating scratches very easily, which shouldn't happen in this high end price range.  Apparently, you are not supposed to wash the rice inside the non-stick cooking pot, because it will scratch the non-stick coating inside.  The user interface is not user friendly, with buttons spread through out the front of the rice cooker interface.  Don't know why there is a lock and unlock (which you have to turn to lock/unlock) feature on the rice cooker, I've never seen that on any other rice cooker.  Thought this rice cooker would be able to make nurungji / crispy - crunchy rice, however it does not work, unless you cook your rice in the nurungji function 3 to 4 times, which takes 3-4 hours.  Otherwise, this is an average rice cooker, which needs a lot of improvements to justify the hefty price.

date: Reviewed in the United States on July 26, 2022
author: Paul Goodman
title: Not good for brown rice or basmati rice. Pressure cooking is a bad idea for rice.
body: After lots of experience with many rice cookers, my requirements for a rice cooker were pretty basic: it must not burn itself up due to faulty electronics, it must not have a potentially toxic Teflon or similar nonstick pot, and it must make decent brown rice. This Cuckoo had some great reviews on various sites, so I splurged and tried it. Big mistake! Now it's too late to return it.The first thing you notice is that it TALKS TO YOU! Loudly. And it talks in Korean. It's unnecessarily difficult to turn off the talking function. The instruction manual is unclear and hard to understand, and turning off the talking is completely non-obvious; it took a while.The big issue is that pressure cooking is simply a bad idea for cooking rice if you don't want it to be sticky and mushy. Non-pressurized cooking works much better. At first, I followed the instructions for brown rice and it came out awfully gooey and sticky. Yuk! I experimented with different water to rice ratios and found that 1.25 to 1, or even 1 to 1, is about right. But still, the rice is never as good as it should be. Soaking the rice before heating doesn't help. I also tried this with basmati rice, and had more problems - almost inedible. It just doesn't work well.The instructions are awful. There are some tantalizing options for "preset" and "multi-cook", but they don't help, and there is zero useful information about them in the user manual.And, this machine is big - it takes up a lot of counter space. Not good.On the plus side, it seems to be quite well designed and made, after you get over the basic problems of functionality. The pot interior has an "X-well Diamond" coating, which somewhat resembles the nonstick diamond surface of Swiss Diamond pans, which are excellent (and very expensive). I like that part.Eventually, I found another rice cooker that actually does meet the basic requirements, as far as I can tell: the Instant Pot Zest https://www.amazon.com/Instant-Zest-Rice-Cooker-Pot/dp/B07TZL8Y3C/. For about $40, you get a NON pressurized rice cooker, which is exactly what you need to make great rice, a ceramic lined nonstick pot, and (according to the manufacturer) overheat sensing, which should prevent overheating and burning. It's pretty small on the counter, and quite simple to use. The small (8 cup) model is more than sufficient for a family of 4.About overheating: I have had TWO Aroma rice cookers fail by overheating to the point that they created large amounts of horrible smoke and might have caught fire if I hadn't unplugged them. I even had the US Consumer Product Safety Commission investigate the first one. No surprise, they never got back to me and did not stop Aroma from selling those defective devices. Some other low price rice cookers have similar complaints from users. Buyer beware!And, no, I will not use a rice cooker with PFOA, PFAS, or any other sort of Teflon-related non-stick liner. Note that even the very expensive Zojirushi rice cookers use those substances, and many users complain about the nonstick stuff flaking off. Not for my family!

author: Brandon E.
title: All flash, no intuition.
body: This rice cooker failed to do the little things well, while making good rice. But not 300$ good rice.My perspective is that I am a decent cook, I like simplicity and intuitive design. I make rice maybe 3 times a week.Size:This rice cooker is way too big for its job. I'm an American so the rice cooker isn't the center of my kitchen. It's another tool I take out of the cabinet. This rice cooker would have to sit on my counter taking up space unused for most of the week.Software:The standard language is Korean. Although you can set it to English, the 300$ machine has no non-volitile memory. Meaning that when you unplug it and put it away, it will talk to you in Korean when you plug it in in a few days. I'm an American who eats rice every once in a while and I don't intend to have this rice cooker plugged in forever like a microwave.For the buyer:Other than these simple oversights, the rice cooker does cook a pretty good pot of rice. But not anything that a 100$ cooker couldn't.For the Manufacturer:This would be a fantastic rice cooker for Korean buyers. If you'd like to enter the American market, consider designing the dimensions so the rice cooker can be stowed in a cabinet. And if the product has a North American electrical socket, the standard language should be set to English. These small oversights make this product difficult to live with.

date: Reviewed in the United States on June 15, 2022
author: DG
title: Nice price, but not sure it was completely new...
body: The way the box came with one loose box strap/band way over to a side, tape on top of box seal looked possibly tampered with, and the electric cord wrapping was loose, messy and uneven as though someone had undone the wire tie, then re-wrapped it.  But we looked the machine over, and it seemed clean, so we set it up and tried it.  First water boil test to check seals took 3 tries to get it work (chalked it up to likely operator error...) and steam came out the way it was supposed to - just through the steam vent.  I let Amazon know, and they offered to replace/refund, but we decided we'd try it for a week, and then see.  (Sort of a gift for Mom, so wanted her to feel good about a new rice cooker.) We started noticing the lid latch didn't always release easily - would take a few attempts.  After about 3 or 4 days, we noticed the outer bottom of the inner pot had an area that had already worn, like it had been rubbed hard and some of the finishing was 'off', size of a quarter, (like a scratched teflon pan), but we'd been very careful not to scratch or ding it, so not sure why.  Decided there were enough questionable things that I called in to Amazon.  They promptly sent a replacement.  We got it yesterday, and comparing it to the first, the 2nd's vent cover - internal area had what looked like a couple of oil stains on the silicone seal - I could excuse it as possibly a test that they did, and it washed off.  The steam pressure weight has a burnt discolored looking area near the inner circle - chalking it up to manufacturing - too tired to clean off another one and go through returns.  But comparing it against the first, the inside of this weight is clean, the first is black inside (q-tip brings off some carbon from the 1st weight.  Not sure that just a week of making rice in the first should do that, but we'll check this 2nd one after a week of making rice.  BIG difference.  1st cooker made the Calrose (short-grain sushi rice) EXTRA sticky, like glutinous mochi rice.  definitely a lot stickier than we'd expected, but we figured that must be the way it works.  We have a really old Cuckoo that's been working, just the inner lid isn't removeable, and it's started to wear, so figured it was time for a new one, and that didn't make rice this sticky.  Well, this 2nd one makes the rice much closer to the old Cuckoo (SRP-0611FA).  So maybe the first CRP-P0609S does have something wrong with it? Or maybe this 2nd one has something wrong with it? Have no idea at this point.1. Inner pot site description says stainless steel.  This is more like aluminum, and doesn't feel like anodized just b/c of the weight - at least my anodized aluminum pots are much heavier for a similar size.2. I'd personally like to upgrade a step or two, but Mom likes the darker color and doesn't feel like it's worth paying more (not her money, but that's just family dynamics...)- Might update this after we've used the replacement for a bit, but hoping there won't be any issues or questions with this one...

If it was like $20 or $30 to gift card credit then I'd understand but $271?We will not purchase from Cuckoo on Amazon again. date: Reviewed in the United States on September 27, 2021
author: KC
title: Delicious rice!
body: It is now 11/18/2024. After 4 years, the cooker is finally starting to show it's true colors BUT never fear! I use the CLEAN function with pot filled with water at full capacity with a tablespoon of vinegar to rid the smell WEEKLY or TWICE a month and it's back to delicious smelling rice! I will never get tired of my rice cooker telling me when the rice is ready. You have to put work in as goes with any appliance such as a toaster oven or stove..etc.Update: 8/9/22: Been over a year now? Rice cooker still going strong. Still makes delicious rice. We lost our measuring cup inside our 60 gallon rice container but never fear! By using my pinky to measure the amount of water needed (measure up to second knuckle line from fingernail), this smart rice cooker can estimate how long it needs to cook the rice for. No regrets! Still worth every penny!Had this rice cooker for over 6 months, and still going strong!  It's SO IMPORTANT to read the instructions as it tells you everything you may need to know about handling this expensive cooker. As soon as I changed the settings to English, everything was a breeze. Also, for great lasting rice, turn the warming function down to the lowest heat setting which is 76 or something. Keeps my rice from turning brown and awesome tasting for over 30 hours. Used almost everyday. Also super easy to clean and like a car, you must keep it clean for a long lasting supply of delicious rice, which is super easy bc its mostly just wiping the inside and clearing it of dropped rice grains, ensuring the rubber does not produce mold.. Most satisfied investment in food products to buy.

date: Reviewed in Canada on July 29, 2021
author: Eldon
title: Don??t go for it if you expected a high quality Japanese rice cooker, they are just not the same.
body: Well, this is my first time to write any product review from my Amazon purchase.  The product earned 2 out of 5 stars across various categories, are pretty much to it failed to meet with my expectations.  I grow up in a traditional Asian family where rice cooker is one of the most important cooking tools in the kitchen.  I have to admit that my family and I are pretty ??serious?? on the type of rice we eat, as well as how the rice cooker could perform.  With a so-called ??smart-rice cooker?? at this price point, all I could say is that IT COULD NOT COOK THE RICE THAT I EXPECTED. No matter which programme I choose, or the amount of water I added, the rice was never soft enough. Compared to my TIGER brand $99 from Costco, this $360 Made in Korea rice cooker just can??t even make a decent porridge.  All I presume is the programme and the high pressure technology could have two advantages. One, quick that I could have my rice done in about 20 minutes. Two, the steam and high pressure is good with the grain rice.Unfortunately, this pricy rice cooker (probably because it was invented and branded from Korean) didn??t have the receipt for the rice to sit enough time to absorb enough water.  In the due course, the rice is for those who like ??harder??.Last but least, when look into details, many plastic parts including the attached accessories are flimsy.  All I could say ??don??t go for it if you expected to have a high quality Japanese rice cooker??, they are just not the same.

date: Reviewed in the United States on July 21, 2021
author: Chan
title: It's Cuckoo, something beyond rice cooker
body: We have been using Cuckoo for a decade, and have to let go of our 10-year old one due to old design flaws (not detachable top, its coating eventually deteriorated). We thought we could survive w/o Cuckoo by using the conventional rice cooker. After three months of using conventional rice cooker (our 12 years old backup rice cooker, Zojirushi), we realized how much we missed perfectly cooked rice by Cuckoo. Hence, we returned back to Cuckoo and there would be to return.Good: speed cooking (within 15 mins, you could have sushi quality rice), perfect rice cooking (every gain of rice is perfectly cooked - hard to describe, but this is actually mind boggling technology!!!), detachable top for easy cleaning (awesome!!!), three language selections (not interactive, but I felt I could talk to this rice cooker), most of all, it could cook "NooRoongJi (burnt or roasted rice???)".Things need improvement: Cooke's top could be fully opened very fast. When I first opened the top, it surprised me - no gradual opening, just full opening instantly. If you place your Cuckoo near the cabinet, your cabinet might have some nicks or dents soon. I have taped the phelt sheet at the cabinet.Overall, this is a revolutionary kitchenware. Highly recommended!!!

We mainly used it for almost-daily rice cooking, as well as hard boiling eggs and making other pressure-cooked dishes. Generally, you can't go wrong if you follow the instructions. If you're pressure cooking meats etc, DO NOT overfill! Again, follow the instructions, and you won't have an endless stream of delicious broth overflowing out of the pressure valve. I was honestly worried about it blowing up, but it thankfully did not, and kept making great rice & other dishes after a couple rounds of thorough cleaning.

body: Update on January 2022:This cooker stopped working properly. The steam comes out before the right time and the cooking stops in the middle. I have to cook again when this happens and the rice doesn??t taste good enough. I think the one I bought is defective.Update February 2021So I do really love this brand and the fluffy rice it can make, but this cooker is giving me a hard time every time when I try to lock the cap. I have to lock and unlock several times till it can finally work. It started to act like this after we had it for about 5 months. I??m disappointed! This is an expensive piece, I expected it to use it for at least 5 years.Original: I love the rice cooker! It makes the rice fluffy and tasty. Cuckoo is one of the best pressure rice cooker brands in Korea, and it definitely deserves its reputation. The flavor of the white rice, brown rice and purple rice is so much better than any other regular cooker could make. The nonstick inner pot makes cleaning a breeze. The only thing people should know is that when you use high heat function to cook rice, the rice at the bottom of the pot will be very hard. It is a special transitional Korean cooking style, and not everyone can enjoy the hard rice. I learned this from several failures of not getting fluffy rice. Anyway, if you love rice, you should try this cooker. I??m so happy I could find it on Amazon!

date: Reviewed in the United States on September 10, 2020
author: Koala Balla
title: Best rice cooker I've used
body: This is my first time purchasing a Cuckoo rice cooker and I'm extremely impressed. There are some minor caveats/things to bear in mind when you buy this, but TL;DR: this is a solid rice cooker and I haven't made better rice with any other rice cooker.First, the design is sleak -- the back w/ rose gold goes well with most of our appliances, and adds a nice bit of accent color to the kitchen. My only complaint about the external design is how thin some of the platic feels to the touch. This doesn't 'feel' like a product in its price point; it feels much cheaper. I could imagine this breaking irreparably if dropped.The built-in voices are fun -- being greeted with a little jingle and hearing a voice notify me of each stage of the cooking process is really nice, in spite of being more of a 'fun' thing than a practical one. The voice is in multiple languages, and can be disabled entirely. Again, a bit over-the-top, but it has its function of offering me the freedom to work about the kitchen without constantly checking the rice cooker.This particular rice cooker is non-stick while avoiding the use of teflon. I do not have burnt rice stuck on the bottom of the bowl -- instead, I get consistent rice to my liking from top to bottom, and I don't need to worry about chips of teflon in my food.It has a self-sanitizing feature, that will use hot water to sanitize itself. It should be noted the self-sanitizing is not self-cleaning. In other words, you still need to detach the various plates and components to sufficiently clean the unit. One should not rely on this feature alone to keep their rice cooker clean and in good condition.Speaking of components, there are multiple components that are detachable for cleaning purposes, specifically in the lid. This is a pressure rice cooker, and the various lids and steam filters need cleaning. This is fine and dandy, but the first plate in the lid really requires some strength. It is held in by a thick rubber gromet with a tiny rubber handle, which you can pull to remove the plate. The rubber handle is small and smooth, so people with big fingers/hands will struggle getting a decent grip on it, and people with smaller fingers and hand may struggle with the amount of force required to detach it.It is areas in the design like this and the plastic enclosure where cuckoo made clear design decisions to save on production costs. Luckily, none of these things detract from the quality of the product it yields.I have NEVER cooked better straight rice out of the rice cooker than I have with this.There are several cooking modes to choose from -- most folks will use the glutinous rice setting, which has 3 settings of its own - normal, high heat, and turbo.Normal mode will cook the rice in around 40 minutesHigh heat will cook the rice in about 30Turbo will cook it in about 20The quality degrades the higher power you use -- but not by much. The worst 'quality' rice I got was using the turbo setting for glutinous rice -- it was only slightly tougher than what I'd have preferred, but if I'm in a pinch for time, the turbo mode yields rice that is good enough.Normal mode seems to have the best yield, as the rice is evenly cooked from top to bottom. There are zero 'hard' pieces of rice, nor a collection of burnt rice on the bottom of the bowl, reducing waste. Each grain is soft and is as sticky as I want.It's important to note there are timed modes, as well as ways to customize each option's cook settings, so you can tweak the presets to suit your needs.All in all, this is one of the best cooking appliances I've purchased in awhile.

date: Reviewed in the United States on September 1, 2020
author: BB
title: Can't decide if benefits outweigh the cost
body: This item cooks rice deliciously.  I've used the GABA function and the Glutinous (short/med grain white rice) function but honestly that's all I know how to make in this.  There are plenty of settings, I'm just unsure how to use them.  And that is where the difficulty lies.I need clarification of the different functions/settings and what modes to use for different grains/rices.  I cook all sorts of rice - long grain, Jasmine, risotto, etc and grains - steel cut oats, quinoa, barley, etc.  I really don't want to take the time experimenting with plain versions of these because the cleaning of the cooker is lengthy.I've scoured the web, checked different model manuals and I'm still coming up blank.  The only phone number I found is for servicing, not customer service.  I'm hoping a company rep sees this and replies.  If not, I'll return it and shop around for a different model/brand.

date: Reviewed in the United States on August 31, 2020
author: M. Smith
title: Easy to use, but make sure you follow instructions
body: This rice cooker makes great rice, it cooks quickly, and thoroughly, and is the most similar to cooking rice on the stove than other rice cookers I've tried. I do add a little extra water than the fill lines recommend to achieve that stickier texture, but it works well without being mushy or soggy.The only issue I've had with this rice cooker is that the removeable silicone ring on the lid has to be placed exactly in the correct position, or else it will not allow the steam to be released properly.There is a removeable part of the lid that has a silicone ring on it that can be removed for cleaning, but after that is removed, there is a separate silicone ring attached to the lid itself that can be removed for cleaning. I popped it back on after cleaning and noticed that the next time I cooked rice, the water leaked out from the handles. When I looked it up online, I realized that I had installed that ring incorrectly, and there are markings that have to line up or else it won't function properly.As long as everything is reinstalled on the cooker properly after cleaning, it's fine, but you just have to be certain to line everything up properly.

date: Reviewed in the United States on August 28, 2020
author: picky Mom
title: A good pressure cooker
body: The one I owned before this was $300 more with more cooking funtions. But it didn't last as long as I wish (about 10 year or so). This cooker is simpler but has essential functions. I had to try several ways to cook rice the way i Iike it..finally  got there. Its a good pressure rice cooker with very reasonable  price. I am happy  with the purchase.

date: Reviewed in Canada on August 13, 2020
author: Amazon customer

body: Love this rice cooker but its louder than expected. When you open the lid, just be careful, its not a smooth as some other rice cookers.

date: Reviewed in the United States on August 12, 2020

body: i usually make rice almost every other day so i eat alot of rice. i had a zirozushi before this but the hinge on the lid broke.  the default settings made the rice a bit more stickier then i liked to i went into the setting and adjusted the temp to make it a bit less stickier. and im glad to report that it makes perfect rice every time. its also makes rice in less then 30 minutes compared to my zirozushi(it took an hour). it also keeps rice warm without it turning yellow and rock hard longer then my zirozushi. overall i love everything about it

date: Reviewed in the United States on July 29, 2020

body: I love my new Cuckoo. I bought this one to replace my current one of 8 years. It??s the new joy in my life. My family love the taste of the rice from this new rice cooker. We eat rice daily and this is one of the best tasting rice we??ve had. It??s true the lid could have been designed with a more gentle opening mechanism but I??m giving it a 5 star for the taste of the rice. It??s half the price of my last one and so I think it??s totally worth it.

date: Reviewed in the United States on July 25, 2020
author: Flannel Pajamas
title: Okay but mostly disappointed.
body: I think this rice cooker is just okay. There's a lot of inconsistencies in the rice results...and if you eat a lot of Jasmin rice, I don't think it is the best cooker for that. It does very well for brown and GABA rice, and short grain. It doesn't do well for that special crunchy bottom rice, which is more chewy than crunchy, and I think not good for my teeth enamel. The pressure unit doesn't seem to work properly. It originally would be silent when cooking, until the pressure release, but now it just cooks with the release valve pretty much open on its own. The inside steel plate that's removable for cleaning has a super short silicone that makes it difficult remove without using a dry cloth for gripping. This same plate also falls into the rice while cooking pretty regularly, so I think that silicone gasket that secures it isn't right during cooking. I hate opening up my rice cooker to see this plate sitting directly on top of my rice. There's many little annoying features that just don't work. I also hate it when my rice turns out grey. It only does it sometimes, so I am not sure why. Maybe I don't wash the rice enough. But I have never had grey rice with any other cooker in my entire life of rice cooking. So I get worried about the chemicals that might be in my grey rice.I have had this cooker for over a month now, and compared to my old fuzzy logic Zojirushi rice cooker, I find the rice better with my old cooker. The good thing about the newer Cuckoo is the speed , and it cooks a good brown and GABA rice much better because of the high pressure. But we don't eat brown that often.I am contemplating on replacing this with a Zojirushi again, and saving this for when I need to make brown rice. Pretty disappointing overall. The Cuckoo does the two things better that my old cooker lacked, but I don't know if it was enough to sacrifice for quality, white (not grey) rice.

date: Reviewed in the United States on July 3, 2020
author: Seung Gin Lee
title: It??s okay
body: This rice cooker does okay with smaller volume but does not do well when using large volume of rice. 2 cups vs. 6cups. This unit doesnt cook rice as well as my other cuckoo rice cooker.

body: After our 12 year-old Zojirushi died, we went for this Cuckoo. A bit hard to navigate, but once you're dialed in, it really goes. The turbo rice function will yield 2 cups of perfect white rice in 12 minutes: 3X faster than the Z.

Was hoping it was within the 1 year warranty and that the seller (Amazon) would assist with a replacement.  Extremely disappointed with this product.

I've been using InstaPot pressure cooker for years, and decided to make an upgrade.In terms of flavor, I don't see a huge improvement that justifies the $200 cost difference from InstaPot. Perhaps I'm still not used to the water level required for this cooker. Only time will tell.With that said, I absolutely love "Keep warm" function on this cooker. The rice barely changes its taste for a full day, and still is tastes decent up for additional day or two. The same function on InstaPot always overcooks the rice afterwards, so I always had to keep the excess rice in a freezer.

date: Reviewed in the United States on April 8, 2020
author: Nicolas
title: How to change voice navigation from default Korean language to English
body: The cooker's voice navigation is in Korean by default.  The instruction to change from Korean to English voice navigation in the manual is not easy to follow and it took me a while to get it done.  These are 4 steps:1. Press 'Auto Clean'  one time to enter changing mode2. Press and hold 'Auto Clean' 3 secs to enter voice change in language. You will see the flashing of '0:  0', which is current setting in Korean voice3. Press 'Menu >' to change from '0:  0' to '0:  1'  for English voice. Now you will hear voice in English.4. Press 'Pressure Cook/ Glutin Turbo'  to save and exit.  Done!

title: Crazy Talking Pressure Rice Cooker...
body: I was a fan of  Japanese rice cookers (Zojirushi, Panasonic, Sanyo and Tiger...).  One day my Sanyo rice cooker died (serving me well for 11 years)...  so decided to buy another Japanese rice cooker.  I have visited sister's house for her birthday and had steamed rice for dinner.  Wow!  the rice tasted so good and texture was so soft and moist (just right!).  I've asked my sister about the rice cooker she is using.  She told me she is using Cuckoo pressure rice cooker.  Never tried a pressure rice cooker so I was in doubt...  I decided to give a shot and bought this rice cooker and oh~~ man~~~  This is a choo choo train!  Drive me nut!  No wonder why they call it "Cuckoo" lol!  A talking pressure rice cooker!  After all the crazy process, the rice was done and ready to serve.  When I put the rice in my mouth... Oh~~ man this taste sooo good!  All my family members loves it (except the 3 mouths old one...  too loud for him...)  Highly recommended!

body: Would give this item 5 stars except is it is missing one critical function, and that is a slow opening top. date: Reviewed in the United States on January 13, 2017
author: Andrew Lee
title: "you aren't going to get the best rice cooked without a Cuckoo"
body: I was told by my parents and their parents and I'm sure their parents told them, "you aren't going to get the best rice cooked without a Cuckoo"!This model is so modern compared to to the last generation of Cuckoo's my parents bought roughly 3 years ago. The brushed aluminum ring is gorgeous, coupled with the black body and orange screen, this is the Rolls Royce of pressure rice cookers. The menu systems can get a little confusing due to the lack of buttons, but having the option to have english is always a plus and makes things a little bit easier. Screen is bright, buttons are bright, 6 cups is a perfect size for a small family or a single home owner like myself. Just a quick tip, if you plan on mixing brown grain with white rice, make sure you use the center column to measure your water. If I'm doing two cups, I always add a little bit more water and it comes out perfectly. Being raised in a Korean American family, I know how serious it is to have well prepared rice lol.If you're looking for an awesome modern rice cooker, this is the best of the best!

date: Reviewed in the United States on December 6, 2016
author: K. Chan
title: My first Cuckoo and I love it.  Great for rice and multi cook.
body: I've used it for several months now.  It works like a charm.  The japanese or korean rice we cook tasted so much better.  If you like stickier rice, this is the right choice.  I also used the multi function frequently and cook chicken, pork chop with onions and garlic.  It tasted really delicious.  The onions melt into a sauce within 45 min of Multi Cook function.  The auto clean button is a life saver.  I used the cooker to cook fish before, it tasted good but left a bad smell afterward.  The auto clean is really easy to use, you pour water up to the 2 cups line and the cooker would auto clean/sterilize for you. After it runs its cycle, any stains can be wipe out clean easily.  This is my first Cuckoo and I highly recommend it.  The black color also makes it look very classy in the kitchen.  It also speaks English and Korean.  It will tell you when it is several minutes away from done cooking.  My daughter also used it to make a cake with the recipe included.  It was a little dry but still tasted good. Love it!

I cooked the rice to test.  The first thing I noticed was the rice in contact with the pan was way overcooked.  It was harder and chewy.  When I tried a bowl, parts of the overcooked chewy rice are mixed in with perfectly steamed rice.  I did not like it as the chewy rice stuck to my teeth like gummies.  My old Zojirushi made perfectly steamed rice every time.

date: Reviewed in the United States on March 31, 2024
author: Mary Smith
title: Really good rice cooker, great quality, very well made, heavier than most of this capacity.
body: So far, it is my favorite rice cooker. I ve had many brands but this is my 4th cuckoo, and am so glad I purchased this one. Not too many unneeded options.This one is legible, can see the markings in the rice pot it's white and stands out from the black finish of their pots. Most of them are all black and I can't see anything but this one! No problem !!Bought as a used item as were all the other purchases I've made on amazon, very minor scratches on heat pad in cooker that's it, it's perfect condition works well and easy to use!!,10 cup cooker is bigger than I would like but quality for this price, hard to beat!!!Seals incredibly tight, great water catch (big) well thought out in design and configuration had similar unit but this unit is superior to 5 qt unit I had returned, so glad I did.Lid doesn't fly up with force as the other units do when opened. Better visibility of controls and it comes with a rice paddle holder.Now if they could include retractable cord and a lid that doesn't jump out at you ! Japanese models are better optioned in that regard.I think it would elevate their user friendly critics. Japanese rice cookers seem more detailed in user functionality and ease of use!For me it'sa 10 +++Unit is 18 lbs I believe, so it is well made !!!Very happy with this item!!!!!!!Highly recommend

body: Purchased in 2020 for $220 and overall great rice cooker. A little on the expensive side, but cooks rice in under 20 minutes with the fast setting. The only thing that I had a problem was when turning to lock/unlock. When I would turn "Lock" the setting wouldn't read it and would flash on "unlock/lock". So, I would have to turn to unlock to lock again for the setting to read it as Locked in order to start cooking. That became an issue and recently just completely broke down on me. I contacted the manufacturer but they said there was nothing they can do since it has been passed the 1 year warranty mark. The guy recommended me to call this other phone number, as they would be able to help out with machine parts -or something. Since then no luck of them answering the phone to help me out. VERY DISAPPOINTED ?????? I've read reviews that this "Lock/Unlock" thing is an issue. So know what you're getting yourself into.

date: Reviewed in the United States on June 6, 2023
author: RCH
title: This is a great Rice Cooker for the money
body: I own two of the Zojirushi Commercial rice cookers but needed more production capability (cooked rice).  I was relooking my options (the Zo is more than $400 for the 20 cup commercial).  I found this Cuckoo model and did a bit of research.  The Cuckoo brand is Korean and is considered to be a top of the line cooker.Pluses:  Half the cost of the Zo Commercial cooker.  50% more production capacity than the Zo  (30 cup vs 20 cup) per cycle.I bought one of these and used it for about 10 days (we do a 50 pound bag of rice every morning).  Both Zo's plus the new Cuckoo helped.  After a week, I ordered a second of these Cuckoos.  (both of these together cost less than a third zo).I really now prefer the Cuckoos to the Zo's.  For us, the capacity of the Cuckoo is perfect (max volume before the lift from the pot becomes too heavy).  The fact that there is a third indicator light (shows cooking, steaming, and warming) rather that the two for the Zo (cook and warm) so you don't have to time your warming light to estimate steaming time.  When the Cuckoo leaves steam and goes to warm, the rice is ready.I can't speak to longevity yet because the short time I have owned them.  I can say they make perfect jasmine, basmati, and sushi style rice, and do it well.v/r rh

date: Reviewed in the United States on April 19, 2025
author: Kevin
title: Game-Changer for Rice Lovers!
body: The CUCKOO CRP-RT0609FW has completely changed how I cook rice and meals. The twin-pressure feature gave me the ability to pick high or soft pressure depending on what I was making. It doesn't matter if it's fluffy white rice or hearty brown rice, it always comes out nice.The 14 menu options are so convenient; I've made GABA rice, porridge and steamed vegetables. The nonstick inner pot is a huge help when it comes to clean-up, and the Auto Clean is an awesome feature that makes it easy to keep clean without extra effort.I liked the Safe Steam Release safety feature, and the 3-language voice guide in English, Korean, and Chinese is pretty nifty and surprisingly helpful.Overall, this rice cooker is sleek, smart, and affordable. It looks great on your counter and cooks like some high quality, restaurant-quality, worth-every-penny kind-of-machine each time. I would definitely recommend this to anybody that wants dependable, high-tech cooking that is easy and enjoyable!

author: A. Einstein
title: [CRP-RT0609FW] Great cooker. This is how to change to English
body: TL;DR: For CRP-RT0609FW only: Step 1: Press [TIME SETTING] for 3 seconds. Step 2: Press [ > ] once. Step 3: Press [HIGH P COOK]. I uploaded an image for clarity.I seen other instructions saying you need to press [Auto Clean] for 3 seconds ?? that didn't work for me.What did work was to press [Time cook] for 3 seconds, then I got a "0:0" on the screen and pressed [ > ] until hearing "This is English" and then press [High Pressure cooking] to confirm. I give this rice cooker 4 stars only because it's so complicated and they seem not care much about the language.

body: The rice cooker works great if your machine actually lasts that long.I have had this rice cooker for about 2 year used it only a handful of time.  The pressure function does  not work as electronic component and the lid lock mechanism are glitchy. The orange light turns on  to use presssure mode if the lid is open all the away and not shut, but if you close the lid the and turn dial to left to lock the  rice cooker, the orange light does not come on. Therefore pressure cooking is not activated for use.  I have to bang on the machine several times then the machine would engage pressure mode and orange light turns on.  The pressure plate and temperature sensors are both clean and free of blockage its definately a mechincal issue. It works great if the orange light actually turn on so can use pressure mode.  This happens everytime I open and close the machine, it like resents or something and does not engage in pressure mode. Warranty is only 1 year which sucks. So expensive to malfunction with very minmal use after only 2 years.  Would not recommend.

date: Reviewed in the United States on November 28, 2023
author: Amazon Customer
title: Too big, too heavy
body: This barely got out of the box. Even though the cooking pot was only 3 quart, as requested, the machine around it was very heavy and complicated looking. This machine is actually oval, even though the pot is round. So it is not compact and round as the pictures [and videos] suggest. It is 13 lbs, for which I would expect something with a lot more volume. SB

date: Reviewed in the United States on October 28, 2023
author: Rachel
title: Amazing!
body: Use mostly for short grain rice and mixed brown/white/glutinous rice. Works great for all! The pressure means you don't have to presoak the rice. Have also cooked sweet potatoes on pressure and worked great and really fast. Keeps rice hot for hours. Many more setting I haven't explored. Only knock would be a rather imprecise and limited recipe book/manual accompanies. Partly why I haven't really explored all settings. Highly recommend.

date: Reviewed in Canada on October 9, 2023
author: Amazon Customer
title: Makes amazing rice, but feels oddly cheap
body: First, this makes amazing rice, 95% as good as the old induction pressure Cuckoo model I used in South Korea.  Sadly, there are two problems.  First, the lid opens so fast that if you don't hold onto it to slow it down, then even when loaded up the front of the cooker lifts off of the counter and it makes a sound like the lid will snap off.  Second, the old level-up one I had in SK had a latch system to hold the inner lid cover on, and that worked very well.  This one instead uses a rubber grommet and nipple.  After I have used this cooker to cook rice, I cannot get enough grip on that little nipple to follow the cleaning instructions on how to remove and clean the inner lid.

date: Reviewed in the United States on April 30, 2021
author: Susan M.
title: Cuckoo Convert
body: For years, I used my Instant Pot to cook rice - it did an OK job but cleaning the stainless insert was usually a bummer, especially when the rice stuck to the pot. I finally decided to try out a proper rice cooker and researched Cuckoo before buying this model. I am so happy with my purchase! I have been using it for about a month now and have zero complaints. It is easy to use, clean, and makes consistently delicious rice.My go-to is brown short grain (sushi) rice or Nishiki's Premium 7 Grains Mix - sometimes, I mix them together. I use the glutinous rice setting for the brown and mixed rice for multi-grain rice/multi-grain + brown combo.The water to rice ratios are pretty much fool-proof: However many Cuckoo-sized cups of rice, then fill to the comparable number water line on the insert (I don't make less than two cups at a time).I tried to give a better idea of size and scale compared to my standard toaster-oven in my pics. When not in use, I store it in a large cupboard over my fridge.The auto clean function is a nice feature - I use it after every preparation.This may be my favorite purchase of 2021 and I have only scratched the surface of what this powerhouse machine can do.

author: Kevin
title: If you want to buy a rice cooker buy Zojirushi!
body: I always wondered which rice cooker is better Japan or Korea? I have always had Zojirushi rice cooker, I have a white neuro fuzzy and a pressure-induction rice cooker. When I tried the Cuckoo, I was highly disappointed. The rice came out clumped together and had a greyish hue. It cooked the rice in less than 30 min, whereas my Zojirushi takes 1 hour. This is due to the Zojirushi soaking rice for 30 min. Using the Zojirushi with the same rice, it come out white and fluffy. The rice also just tasted bad, it was hard to break apart. Also when opening the rice cooker, the front legs lifts up making a thud noise. I also find that all the buttons on it makes it more complicated, the Zojirushi rice cooker is much more simpler and easy to use.

I could understand if I had it for a year or so but this was just used about a month and it is starting these four distinct shapes of scratches!!! There is gotta be an issue with the rice cooker or the coating of the inner pot. A design fault!!!  I am just so disappointed at the rice cooker. It does its job cooking the rice so Im not sure what to do????????

date: Reviewed in the United States on February 27, 2023
author: Joyce G
title: high-quality
body: The CUCKOO CRP-ST0609F is a high-quality rice cooker that combines the power of pressure cooking with the convenience of a warmer. With a 6-cup (uncooked) capacity, this rice cooker is perfect for smaller families or for those who want to cook rice quickly and easily.One of the key features its twin pressure system. This system allows the rice cooker to cook rice quickly and efficiently, which is perfect for busy families who want to prepare meals quickly. Additionally, the cooker has a warming function that helps to keep the rice warm and fresh until it's time to serve.The rice cooker also features a non-stick coating, which makes cleaning up a breeze. This is especially useful for busy families who don't have a lot of time to spend on cleaning up after meals.Another great feature of the  CRP-ST0609F is its user-friendly interface. The digital display is easy to read and navigate, and the buttons are clearly labeled and easy to use. Additionally, the cooker comes with a variety of pre-set programs, making it easy to cook a variety of different types of rice, including white rice, brown rice, and more.Overall, this rice cooker is a great choice for anyone who wants a high-quality rice cooker that is easy to use, efficient, and reliable. With its twin pressure system, non-stick coating, and user-friendly interface, this rice cooker is sure to be a hit with busy families who want to enjoy delicious, perfectly cooked rice with minimal effort.

The rice comes out perfect and consistent every time.The pressure cooker does get a little loud when it??s releasing pressure, but it??s not a deal breaker. It does play a cute train noise.

date: Reviewed in the United States on January 9, 2023
author: gokiburi
title: Moderately priced compromise
body: I bought this on a impulse during the Black Friday sales but ultimately returned due to a defect in the pot. Interesting concept and a relatively moderate price point for these products even at regular price. May be a good choice for someone with limited space wanting to occasionally use for a non rice use. The pressure plate and pot was really bouncy on the unit I got which was annoying.  Also, you would need to be very careful releasing the lid as it does open with force almost upending the entire unit. May be difficult to do if also trying to avoid the steam from inside the unit. Weirdly, the newer type screen seems to scare my pets. May have been the very large oval on the screen along with round knobs. Might have been interpreted as an eye? Anyway with the open floor plan I had to completely remove from the living area to keep the peace until it was returned. Ultimately, I went with their multi cooker with the non coated pot and a separate IH pressure rice cooker since I have the space. These units are ??older?? type units however build quality does seem a bit nicer. Also no one seems to have an issue with these units. Spare parts are also readily available. Always a something to look for  with these things.Update: forgot to mention that this does not ship with a steamer tray. Small thing if you never intend to use this for anything other than rice. If not, its an annoyance since it is not an accessory readily available on the manufacturer??s website and this is not an IP so you will need to be careful with another option.

author: Go Gators!
title: Loving this Rice Cooker!
body: Really loving this rice cooker so far.  We tried the 3 different modes for rice 1st Pressure/Turbo, 2nd Pressure and 3rd Non Pressure.  The non pressure cooker is how I'm used to rice cooked and it came out great.  The Pressure/Turbo cooked the quickest and the rice was good.  I'm not sure what the benefit of just cooking it on Pressure without the turbo.  The rice came out fine.  The best consistency was with the non-pressure (regular asian rice) cooking method.  The warmer lasted quite a long time, at least 20-24 hours.  I don't think I'd go over that, the rice gradually turns yellow.  I like the nonstick diamond coated pot.  It makes washing really easy.  Taking off the inner lid to clean is a bit more difficult, the stick is pretty good to the exterior lid.  But once you get it it's easy to clean.

author: HouseCat
title: THIS IS THE BEST RICE COOKER! MUST BUY!!
body: If you are looking for a Cuckoo rice cooker (you already know this brand is great!), this one has one of the most safety measurements, induction heating, pressure cooking, broad cooking functions, can speak Eng/Kor/Chi, and stainless steel coated pot and lid. I did lots of comparisons between models and brands before I decided to get this. 6-cup is good for a family of 3-4. If your family is bigger, consider getting a 10-cup. If you don't need to use that many functions, you can just throw rice and water in and press Pressured Cooked. Done!ANYONE HAVE A PROBLEM WITH YELLOW OR SOGGY RICE, be sure that you open the lid as soon as the rice is cooked and stir it thoroughly to let the moisture/water out. After that, you can close the lid and let the rice stays until you are ready to eat. You basically have to stir the rice like that with any cookers anyway. I tried the rice once after 5 hours and it was still really great. My family has multiple cookers and sometimes they can keep the rice for days.I got this rice cooker at a such amazing deal from Amazon Warehouse! Hope it will stay for years.

date: Reviewed in the United States on May 5, 2017
author: Joy
title: LOVE IT!
body: I love my rice cooker. It looks amazing. Everyone has commented on how expensive it is, but it's well worth the price. I haven't tried cooking anything on here besides rice. I love congee and sadly, this rice cooker doesn't cook congee correctly. There is a porridge button specifically for congee but when the rice was done, it was just super soft and fluffy. I like my congee to have a more watery consistency and with the extra water I added to the pot before cooking, it still doesn't work. I still use the porridge button because when my mother cooks congee, sometimes she makes it watery and sometimes she doesn't, so I've had both ways. When you first start the rice cooker, it is in Korean but you can change the setting to English. It has a built in battery for emergencies like a power outage or if you accidentally unplug the machine while it's cooking, it will resume its cooking when it's plugged back in. I usually keep my rice cooker unplugged and each time I plug it back in, the English setting is still there. Make sure to check out where the rice cooker is made from because I've noticed the cheaper Zojirushi/Tiger brand is made from China. I was told by my mother to never buy a rice cooker that is made from China because they break down faster than the original one. Well if I'm going to spend over $100 on my rice cooker, I might want to make sure it last for a long time.

date: Reviewed in the United States on October 16, 2015
author: Molly
title: English cooking manual not included
body: I received the rice cooker promptly and it is a gorgeous rice cooker.  However, although the standard manual comes in both English and Korean, the cooking manual is only in Korean.  If I'm paying $500 for a cooker that claims to make bread, soups, porridge and other complete meals, it should come with English instructions as to how to cook these meals otherwise we are left with just cooking rice.  I highly suggest the manufacturer include this in future models as I think it would make a huge difference when people decide to purchase these expensive devices.

Regular pressure cooker heat comes from bottom only compare to ih pressure cooker as a whole. Rice taste veryGood, and took like 18 minutes for quick cook option. date: Reviewed in the United States on July 21, 2015
author: Madison Stemm
title: Amazing rice cooker! Cooks sweet, sticky rice super fast!
body: UPDATE: I've had this for half a year now and I still love it. I've almost always got rice cooking or sitting in Keep Warm in the Cuckoo, and I've still had no issues. I love how fast it is with plain sticky rice, and the GABA setting for brown rice makes brown rice that tastes *awesome*. I haven't tried the dough fermenting setting yet, but I think I'm going to this weekend. It's definitely something I'm interested in.At any rate, I wholeheartedly recommend this rice cooker! It's one of the biggest workhorses in my kitchen, and I really don't have any big complaints with it after 6 months. If you're willing to spend a big chunk of cash on a rice cooker, you won't be disappointed with this one!*******tl;dr: I was torn between this and the Zojirushi induction heating pressure cooker rice cooker, so I bought both. I liked the Cuckoo better! I liked the tactile buttons, low spatial profile, and dimpled scooper with the Zojirushi, but the rice from the Cuckoo cooked faster, came out sweeter and stickier, and the bowl was much higher quality (from my understanding, the Japanese version of the Zojirushi cookers have a similar high-quality bowl, but the American versions use a thinner bowl). The Cuckoo also lets you customise the end-product of your rice a lot more! You can't go wrong with either brand, but the Cuckoo won me over in the end.I don't write reviews very often, but I love this cooker and I wanted to add my two cents about it!I've had a low-end micom Zojirushi (the Zojirushi NS-LAC05XA) for something like four years. Before I bought it, I'd only used the cheap Black & Decker and Aroma rice cookers that burn out after a year, and dry out rice after it's been sitting for a few hours. The micom Zojirushi was an absolutely incredible upgrade! Suddenly, my rice came out evenly cooked, soft, fluffy, and lasted for DAYS on "keep warm", which was amazing since I was living alone and it was annoying cooking small portions of rice at a time.For that record, that Zojirushi is still going strong, and I'm going to be giving it to my parents, who still just use a Black & Decker rice cooker. I still maintain that was probably the best kitchen purchase I ever made as a college student! I just upgraded because I wanted a larger rice cooker, and I was really curious to try out an induction heating and pressure cooker!Anyway. Once I knew I wanted an induction heating rice cooker, I started agonising about which one to get! I'd never heard of Cuckoo before, but it seemed to have a strong following. From what I could tell, they had been in the pressure cooker business for years, whereas Zojirushi was a relative newcomer to that technology. It looked like an all-around fantastic rice cooker (not to mention looking properly futuristic with that white and steel body!) and had some cool modes (like nurungji cooking mode, so I could make proper sungnyung without using a pot!). The equivalent Zojirushi (NP-NVC10) had a similar set of features, though I couldn't find any information comparing the two. I was torn. I'd developed a bit of brand loyalty to Zojirushi from my experience with their rice cooker and thermoses, but the Cuckoo seemed really interesting. What was I supposed to do?I bought them both, of course!So after using the two for a few weeks, here's what I have to say comparing them:CUCKOO PROS:* FAST. Regular white rice cooks in under half an hour (and it even has a TURBO MODE that cooks it even faster, more like 15 minutes!!) whereas the Zojirushi takes over an hour. The rice isn't necessarily better with the Zojirushi, either. It comes down to preference, but it's less sticky, more granular, and the flavour is a little more grainy, but I think it's less sweet. Personally, I prefer the Cuckoo's rice flavour.* STICKY. I like sticky rice, it makes it easier to eat with chopsticks, it's chewier, I just like the texture more. Rice in the cuckoo is a lot stickier and softer.* ADJUSTABLE. Every cooking mode (glutinous rice, sushi rice, GABA brown rice, nurungji, etc) all have the additional "My Mode" customisation that lets you adjust the heated soaking and steaming times to make rice sweeter or softer! This lets you dial in rice to your preferred texture, and it's fantastic.* DURABLE. The Cuckoo's pot is a LOT more heavy duty than the Zojirushi pot, which feels paper thin by comparison. I don't know if that results in better rice, but thicker cast-iron metal would definitely result in more even heat distribution, so I'd assume that would reflect in the rice. As a more damning point, the Japanese version of the Zojirushi model evidently has a much thicker pot. It sounds like this American release may have a thinner pot for more cost-effective manufacturing (and most people probably wouldn't know any different anyway.* INFORMATIVE. The Cuckoo has a really nice touch during the cooking process: it updates when it begins steaming the rice, when it releases the steam, and when rice is complete. The Zojirushi just tells you when the rice is done!* CONSIDERATE. The Cuckoo goes into "Night mode" after 9pm, when the brightness on the screen reduces, and the volume of the voice lowers. That's really nice!! The cable also winds up underneath the body so you don't have excess cable floating around your counter, something the Zojirushi doesn't have. ALSO, the pot has plastic handles, whereas you'd be better off waiting for the Zojirushi pot to cool down before you try and pick it up to clean. Trust me, I've burnt myself.* CLEAN. The Cuckoo has a fantastic option to auto-clean and sterilise itself. I used this when I first got it, and I'll probably be using it once a month to make sure everything is all clean. On top of that, there's a little spill-over reservoir in the back for collecting condensed water that drips down when you open the lid. That's nice!* VERSATILE. Besides being able to happily cook oats (something the Zojirushi's warranty official advises against), it even has an option to act as a regular pressure cooker! Considering an electric pressure cooker is usually at least one or two hundred dollar, this is fantastic.ZOJIRUSHI PROS:* TACTILE. Physical buttons! That's a gimme. Definitely not a dealbreaker, but it makes me happy.* CLEANER. The included rice scoop is SO MUCH BETTER. It has little bumps on the spoon so rice doesn't stick to it. It's way better at non-stick than the Cuckoo's scoop.* EFFICIENT. Extended Keep Warm mode. After 12 hours, the temperature drops so the rice doesn't dry out.  You can manually adjust the keep warm temperature on the Cuckoo, and I believe it reduces the temperature overnight, but the manual isn't entirely clear on that. Actually, the Cuckoo's manual isn't that great in general, which leads me to my next point:* LOCALISED. Much proper English manual. The Cuckoo's manual is kinda sloppy Engrish (with some funny things, like one point where it informs you that you can adjust the voice to be "woman, English, or Chinese"), and the recipes in the back are all in Korean =( The Zojirushi's manual is a proper localisation, with English (!) recipes! The recipes seem more westernised though.* STRAIGHTFORWARD. Simpler cleaning. The steam lid has a push-release button, whereas the Cuckoo's lid has to be unscrewed.* ADORABLE. Cuter notifications. When the cooking begins and ends, it plays 'Twinkle Twinkle Little Star'. The Cuckoo just informs you that rice cooking will now begin, and plays drums when steam release starts, and then plays some Cuckoo theme song when the rice is ready. It's kinda weird, but cute too.* MODE-IER. Adjustments to make rice harder/softer/normal (I think this uses variable pressure settings). I wish the Cuckoo had this! Other modes include rinse-free rice, steam-reduce rice, and umami mode -- but in my comparisons, bumping up the soak time to level 3 in My Mode seems to be the same as Umami Mode!* SMALLER. Lower profile body, easier for storageOverall, the Cuckoo definitely suits my needs more, by far. When you get right down to it, I like the way the rice tastes more, I like all the possible adjustments, I like the higher-level visibility of cooking progress, and I LOVE how much faster it is. The Zojirushi has lots of nice little touches, that make it a better operational experience, but the Cuckoo definitely seems like the superiour rice cooker. Hands-down, between the two I'd recommend Cuckoo every time!...I'm going to miss hearing Twinkle Twinkle Little Star, though =(

date: Reviewed in the United States on November 9, 2014
author: evawen
title: Amazing rice cooker...
body: I bought first a top of the line japanese rice cooker but was not happy with the quality of the item and taste of the brown rice result. so we look around and came across with this new korean rice cooker and it is an amazing product. looks so elegant and yields very good quality result when cooking rice. we first tries gaba brown rice and I was very pleased with the taste. this is the kind of rice cooker you need if you are looking for the best. it is really amazing.  i recommend the 10 cups size if you would like to use it for cooking other than rice.

date: Reviewed in the United States on December 11, 2014
author: Frank & Celia Zou
title: Awesome Cuckoo Rice Cooker!!!!
body: My wife, who is Korean, had long talked about wanting to get a new rice cooker.  Many of her friends had a Cuckoo brand and loved them, so with Christmas coming up I figured this would be a great chance to get her one.  Although this had no ratings yet, and was a new model, I took a chance on it and got this one for her.  I tend to have a history of buying her things that she wonders what I was thinking about when I got it for her, but with this I hit a grand slam!  We installed Cuckoo's app on her phone and set the thing up that way, which took only a few short minutes to do.  Now I will say that when you plug this thing in it will speak to you in Korean, so for the non-Korean speakers don't be surprised.  You can set it to English if need be, so don't let that put you off.  The front panel is in English as well, so you won't get lost in trying to figure out the controls.  She finally cooked her first batch of rice today and couldn't get enough of it.  She is fairly picky about the quality of her rice, but this thing blew her away with how good it came out.  Although this thing is pretty expensive for what many would claim is just a rice cooker, my reply would be that not all rice cookers are created equal.  This machine is made to get the most out of the rice you cook, and on that point it is top notch.  Unlike our old cookers that cook from the bottom, this thing does it differently, so the rice comes out much better.  For those that eat rice regularly, like my wife does, you will love this cooker, or most any of the Cuckoo brand cookers.  For the person who only eats it every so often, this would most likely be an overkill and not worth the money.  My wife's previous cooker was 10 years old, so I expect her to keep this for just as long as that one, so for me the money was very well spent.  Given the different types of rice my wife makes, this thing just can't be beat.  Her old one did a satisfactory job on them, but this one takes rice cooking to a whole new level.  My wife and I couldn't be happier with this!!

date: Reviewed in the United States on July 23, 2015
author: ccpff
title: Hi-tech, high price, but super high quality rice cooker
body: Our old rice cooker was on its last legs after 16 years, so we saved up to get one of these new hi-tech rice cookers. We were wrestling between a Cuchen and a Cuckoo - and from what we were told, they are very comparable. We did read some reviews that stated the latch on the Cuchen that holds the inner lid piece did have a tendency to break, so we opted for the Cuckoo, which holds the inner lid with a circular metal latch.The instruction manual is horrible and a pain to decipher. Takes some focus and effort - but eventually one figures out how to use this thing. Be aware - it comes out of the box speaking Korean - so you will want to navigate to English. Most of the useful rice cooking functions are easily accessed and most others we won't even bother looking at.The rice does cook very fast and well. The induction heating along with the pressure cooking works wonders compared to our old machine. The same rice we have been cooking for years came out more flavorful and with much better texture. Definitely made a believer out of me.

date: Reviewed in the United States on January 7, 2017
author: Hermit
title: Mind-blowing rice from a brilliantly designed machine!
body: If you enjoy rice and perfectly prepared rice (I mean PERFECTLY), this amazing machine is the one. I have never tasted such incredible rice in my life, and I have had a couple of higher end Japanese fuzzy logic machines over the years.The machine is well built and looks like a beautiful, curvy space ship complete with a blue LED control panel. It's extremely well-made, with solid, hefty stainless components. The inside pot is also substantail. I am truly blown away. I would love to see more of these in the US market -- they are remarkable and far exceed their competition.It even speaks! The voice helps you navigate, as the machine is rather complex. The default language is Korean, so I recommend changing the language as your first step if you are an English speaker (it's not intuitive to do this, so reference the manual).PROS: Life-changing rice goodness! It's well made and cooks brown, mixed, white, sushi, glutinous and gaba rice. It also rises and bakes bread and cooks soup and porridge. A count-down indicator lets you know when your rice will be done cooking. The voice provides helpful information and has day and night volume controls. Settings are highly customizable, from cooking speed to rice softness. The operation is quiet except when steam is vented near cooking completion (you hear a short burst as steam releases). And, it has a cleaning cycle. It's also easy to clean manually. It's a machine you can grow into as you learn its various functions.CONS: This is not for someone who enjoys an on/off cooker experience. I think reading the manual is critical to understand this sophisticted machine -- and the manual is not always understandable as there are translation errors or sentences that are not entirely clear. So there will be learning and fiddling (though I think that's half the fun). There aren't a lot of supplemental resources online either, so you need to be the adventurous type if you want to make more than simple white rice. It's also an expensiv machine. While I think it's well worth the investment, if you honestly cannot distinguish between well-prepared and regular old cooked rice, this machine would not be worth your time or money.

date: Reviewed in the United States on December 3, 2020
author: AT
title: Treat the inner pot like it is made of precious metal - there is no replacement.
body: After 1-1.5 years of relatively infrequent use the inner pot got scratched. it is impossible to use it now. The unit otherwise works perfectly. Very good product.Anyways, I have tried to get a hold of someone knowledgeable and helpful at the service department since march of 2020. All my inquiries are about replacement inner pot for model CHSS1009FN. They do not have replacement pots. Every time I call the service someone would complain of how COVID-19 affected them, get my phone number and nobody ever calls back. For 10 months I am trying to get a new inner pot, or even two of them. Calling, calling, e-mailing USA office, Korean office. I can't count how many messages I left on their voice mail. I asked them to order the replacement directly from Korea, I offered to pay the shipment expenses and even $300 for two inner pots. I have a functional unit which I purchased for $650 at home which I cannot use. I just can't throw it in the trash.If the seller reads this message - please help.To potential buyers - be careful with inner pot cleaning, the item scratches easily and there is no replacement for that.

Once I had some of the rice it made I felt like I just had a happy ending in my mouth. It's texture is perfect, the rice is so moist and yet crisp like shrimp shells. Then it stared talking to me in Korean. I quickly got a Rosetta subscription and learned it's native tong. After having it for 6 months we fell in love. I got it a green card and that's when the problems started. It got a tone with me, and then left a few months later. I'm looking desperately for my sweet Cuckoo. date: Reviewed in the United States on January 20, 2015
author: Ellabella
title: Rolls Royce of Rice Cookers
body: I have always had the basic Zojirushi rice cookers.  I decided to upgrade since my old rice cooker was just not up to par anymore.  I just made my first batch of rice in this today and it is the best rice I have ever tasted!  I made a mix of white rice and brown rice.  Not all rice cookers are created equal as the prior reviewer states and he is right!  The heating element is not only on the bottom but around the sides as well.  The cleanup is super easy since the inside is all in one piece with no crevices to clean under.  The under-lid comes apart for easy cleaning.  The inside pot is sturdy and thick.  This rice cooker is quite large and oval shaped.  This is definitely pricey but well worth it for making rice and other dishes since this is also a pressure cooker. The controls are easy to navigate and this makes the most perfect rice ever!  Looking forward to many years of deliciousness.

It almost looks like someone used this product already and returned it. For $500 I expect it to be in perfect condition and to look exactly like the picture provided. Not impressed. I'm probably going to return this product.

title: Fancy!
body: I just started using this rice cooker, but so far it's awesome!  It is pretty huge (as expected of a 10 cup rice cooker) but still surprised me, I guess I expected something a little rounder from the picture (it's a little longer than expected, but definitely not a problem).  I'm still trying to work out all the settings (the instruction book is long) but I expected that from such a complicated product.  If you want a simple no frills rice cooker, this isn't for you (but you probably wouldn't be considering that sort at this price point).  However, if you're looking for something that will cook many things (there's a bread cooking function!), is attractive and easy to clean, then this may be for you!

I have permanently retired my old rice cooker (obviously) as well as my crock pot and pressure cooker as this Cuckoo does everything those did, but better.

date: Reviewed in the United States on June 6, 2020

body: This one is clearly overpriced by the name "full stainless steel".I got confused by the photo image but the body is not made with "metallic" material but "plastic".Of course, only metallic part is the bowl itself same as many other rice cooker.Then, what is the meaning of "full stainless steel"? Highly misleading naming (I should say "cheating" naming)Remember that there is no such "half stainless steel" All stainless steels are same.. only differed by Cr contents.

title: SpaceShip? or Cooker?
body: I give 5+ for its functionality and look.This is our 2nd Cuckoo Rice Cooker which we are replacing after 7 yrs of usage.First, looks is very slick and reminds of high-tech gadget. It fits our kitchen well under the cabinet.Surface has is a bit of grove so I think it won't scratch while cleaning. Inside pan is a bit heavier than last one.In addition, it has NFC which you can use your Android phone - download app - and set few things initially.However, I found this NFC is least used feature.Except those changes, basic functionality was pretty much same.However, I have noticed it keeps rice wormer much longer and keep it fresh.And it goes to automatic energy saver quite nicely.But, for the value, you might get better value with lower priced one.I feel this is a bit over priced as they have packed features that I don't use much.for sure, it does produce a quality rice cooked ( Sticky rice ) and my Korean mother-in-law loves it.

date: Reviewed in the United States on April 2, 2016

author: JL
title: The rice cooker is versatile and multi-functional
body: I recently purchased the CUCKOO CRP-CHSS1009FN Induction Heating Pressure Rice Cooker and Iâ€™m extremely satisfied with my purchase. This rice cooker is made of high-quality materials and is built to last. The induction heating technology completely surrounds the inner pot, making the cooking process quick and efficient. The energy-saving smart algorithm automatically detects grain type and internal temperature to take the guesswork out of perfectly cooked rice. The eco-friendly stainless steel inner pot with X-Wall nonstick coating ensures optimal temperature and heat distribution throughout the cooking process and is also ultra-durable.The CUCKOO CRP-CHSS1009FN Induction Heating Pressure Rice Cooker is complete with LED touch controls, 3-language voice guide (Korean, Chinese, English), a preset timer, a reheat option for leftovers, and a keep-warm function to ensure none of your foods are ever overcooked while serving. The rice cooker is versatile and multi-functional, with 19 menu options including GABA rice, brown rice, glutinous/white rice, sushi rice, sticky rice, pressure cook, multi-cook, and bread baking along with auto-clean to effortlessly deep clean your cooker.

title: Love it!
body: I rarely leave reviews; but I had to for this product??I LOVE IT! Lol??honestly, it makes my life SO much easier when it comes to cooking my rice and especially a perfect bowl of oatmeal. However, I couldnt give it a 5 star rating because the menu/option interface is NOT intuitive at all. I speak Korean, and it was somewhat difficult to understand some of the menu options, so I just played around with it until I found an option that made sense lol and switched the voice to English. Also, you would think the Porridge option would be the correct option to cook oatmeal right?! WRONG??.i learned this the hard way; after I selected porridge and seconds later, oatmeal goo was all over my rice cooker and spraying all over my counter top????; I later found out, when cooking oatmeal, you must use the ??Glutinous Rice?? option??.who wouldve guessed that?! I found that out from a youtuber! Because nothing in the instructions mentioned that. Not even in Korean. Atleast, none that Ive found so far??other than that??Iam so happy with my purchase; because I personally LOVE a good bowl of oatmeal, and this cooks it to perfection!

date: Reviewed in the United States on August 20, 2019
author: Charles NYC
title: IN THIS ESSENTIAL WAY, IT BEATS ZOJIRUSHI
body: In America, Zojirushi has been the well-deserving gold standard in rice cookers for a long time, and I had used their highest end pressure cooking model for quite a few years with excellent results. But I had one serious regret after buying it, all because I didn't check the specs before purchase: Zoji's very expensive pressure rice cookers are essentially no faster than the much cheaper non-pressure models. White Japanese rice is still close to an hour, brown rice almost 90 minutes and GABA rice clocks in at about 3.5 hours. I ended up making rice--especially brown and GABA--much less often that I wanted because the cooking time would delay dinner too long, especially during the work week.When I first looked at the specs for this Cuckoo Pressure Rice Cooker, two things blew me away: first, the price! Wow--it makes Zoji look like a bargain! But second, the SPEED: white rice in under a half-hour (with an even faster "Turbo speed" available) and brown rice in about 50 minutes. (GABA, however, is not that much faster at 3 hours.) For that kind of speed--which meant I would use my rice cooker a lot more often--I decided to sell my Zoji and take the price plunge on the Cuckoo. And it was SO worth it!The first thing I noticed was build quality--the Zoji was perfectly fine, but this Cuckoo is built like a tank and it's big. The smaller 6 cup size, which I have, is as big or bigger than the Zoji 10-cup. As for speed, it was sometimes even faster than the specified times.But speed would mean nothing if either taste and texture were compromised here, and that's not the case here. In fact, the GABA brown rice is the best rice I've ever tasted at home, period. As for white and brown rice, I'd call it a draw with the Zoji. Some may have a preference for one or the other, but that will be a matter of personal taste rather than a true qualitative difference between the two machines.So why four stars and not five? There's one area where Zoji trounces the Cuckoo. Because Zoji has been serving the American market for so long, it's easy to find tried and true instructions for cooking virtually any kind of rice you want to prepare. Cuckoo, by comparison, is a relative newcomer to the U.S. market, so that kind of information is non-existent for their cookers. I've been using Zoji instructions for wild rice and other non-Asian varieties, but results are hit and miss because they are very different machines. Another sign of this newness to the American market is the English language instruction manual, where the clarity of directions leaves a lot to be desired. I'm pretty technically minded and still found the learning curve steep. Once you get the English language navigation on the rice cooker turned on, it gets a lot easier--but just changing the language requires googling for instructions on how to do that.So there you have it: the mostly excellent summary of what you get with this Cuckoo rice cooker, along with its few flaws. Whether it and its price tag are for you is only something that you can decide. I hope you find my review helpful in making a buying decision.
