date: Reviewed in the United States on January 10, 2018
author: Rick
title: Excuse me stewardess... I speak Korean
body: This one requires a translator.  Seriously!  Just to restate, this is a rice cooker.  I found the operation and controls not at all intuitive.  The instruction manual reads as though they simply copied the Korean instructions and pasted into Google translator.  Much of the instruction doesn't make sense because of the bad translation.  No kidding, it will take you at least 5-10 minutes just to figure out how to get the cooker to stop yelling at you in Korean.  As far as the rice goes, it was good but didn't find it to be any better as far as texture and quality compared to my 10 year old Panasonic.  I primarily purchased this for white rice and to cook steel cut oats in the morning.  THIS WILL NOT COOK STEEL CUT OATS (SCO) via the porridge setting!  Regardless of the ratios of oats to water I could not keep the contents from overflowing.  Each attempt resulted in a huge mess all over the cooker and my countertop.  Apparently, PRESSURE rice cookers cannot handle steel cut oats on the porridge setting.  This is confirmed on Zojirushi's website.  If you're looking for a rice cooker that can handle SCO, check out Tiger's JKT.  It's half the cost, has a dedicated SCO option (reviews indicate it makes great oats) and it makes excellent rice... among other things.  It also looks to be a lot more intuitive/easier to operate and you don't have to deal with a computer yelling at you in Korean.

date: Reviewed in the United States on December 17, 2017
author: EL
title: The best rice cooker I have ever experienced
body: The best rice cooker I have ever experienced! As it's price tag says, this is the best, two hands down. I used to own multiple rice cookers from  "Zojirushi" and I always thought their rice cooker is the best but this changed my opinion completely. The rice comes out so silky and tender yet firm enough to feel each grain rolling around on your tongue. It is just amazing how this rice cooker makes the rice like that!

date: Reviewed in the United States on November 21, 2017
author: Rena M.
title: Great Rice Cooker! If you are into good quality rice
body: This is a great rice cooker! I had a $400 Japanese one for five years before I switched to this one. This uses real pressure to cook the rice, so the rice is cooked much faster. My previous one took minimum of 35 min for fast cooking (white rice), and at least 45 for normal cooking (white rice). This one only takes 15 min for fast, and 30 min for normal cooking. I am very happy with the cooker. However, the rice seems to be more sticky due to the cooking method, so I had to try out several different method (water volume, setting, etc) to find the rice I want.When I received the product, the inner pot was scratched and the steam plate was missing (sold by Amazon.com LLC). I contacted CuckooWorld.com to get a new inner pot and steam plate, since I did not want to return the whole thing. The representatives were very courteous, and while it was a little bit of a hassle to get them replaced, they finally shipped them out. Overall, nice experience and I would definitely recommend this to anyone who likes good quality rice.

date: Reviewed in the United States on August 8, 2017
author: Otterindawata
title: Updated review
body: It's Beautiful! This rice cooker has an excellent sturdy pot with lots of room for larger rice batches. I generally use about 3 gou a day which is above the 2 gou minimum cook of this unit. The voice is quite cute but I prefer that this machine works in stealth mode. Though the price is steep and the instruction manual looks to be done by google translate the simplicity and direct nature works wonderfully. This item is quite large in comparison to the actual cooking pot. As far as cleaning is concerned I'm going to handwash everything with a sponge to protect the coatings.Update=> I've used this for steel cut oats, and it works WONDERFULLY. 1 gou of steel cut oats and this puppy had everything tasting perfectly after 80 minutes. I used to cook these in a slow cooker overnight but this cooker pushed out a wonderfully textured steel cut oats batch with ease. I will note that you should dial the amount of oats in for your intended texture. For my next batch I will probably use more oats per minimum fill in order to get firmer oats but the increase won't be more than 1/8 of a gou ( a couple tablespoons).UPDATE TO THE UPDATE... So YEAH the oats decided to get messy and slimy completely clogging the dewtray.. so the oat cooking days have ended. However sushi rice, mixed beans and rice, bread proofing.... AMAZING. I will say that though cleaning the nooks and crannies can be tiresome the quality of cooking pays into profits... I am so very glad I made this investment.

But for everyday use by one or two people, the 3-cup model is ideal.I took photos comparing my old 6-cup and the new 3-cup. While the exterior size of the 3-cup is slightly smaller, its inner pot is definitely more compact.I??ll keep my old 6-cup unit for holiday gatherings while enjoying my new 3-cup every day.

date: Reviewed in the United States on April 23, 2023
author: Helen
title: Review after 5 years daily use; how to replace inner pot/gasket
body: Initially purchased this around 2018. It's not perfect, but is the best out of all the rice cooker I've tested. Jump to the end of the review if you just want to know about replacing the inner pot/gasket.This size is perfect for singles/couples. I live alone, and make 3 cups of rice every other day. After I make rice, I leave it on keep warm mode in perpetuity - so that I have access to warm rice at a moment's notice.It's got lots of features, but it doesn't really matter to me. I only use it to make short grain white rice (Korean/Japanese "sticky" rice). I have also used a cheaper non-pressure Cuckoo rice cooker in the past. It's ok, but the rice doesn't come out as soft due to lack of pressure, which is very important to me.I have also tried a Zojirushi pressure rice cooker. It made comparable rice, BUT the stainless lid started to RUST within few days of use. It's probably fine if you are a normal person that occasionally eat rice, and don't leave it on warm mode all the time. To me, this was a dealbreaker.After 2 years of using the Cuckoo every day, the inner pot started to peel, and moisture would collect inside the rice cooker, due to the aging of the rubber gasket.This is expected.Replacement inner pot can be found on ebay, for about $60. The rubber gasket (called "packing") for about $20. With international shipping from Korea, it'll run you about $90-$100 in total.Search for the model number EHSS0309F, or the Korean equivalent EHS0320F (same rice cooker, just different voltage). The rubber gasket ("packing") model is CCP-DP03.Make sure to replace BOTH the inner pot and the rubber gasket. Otherwise, the steam will continue to leak.So, you can expect to spend $100 in replacement parts every 2 years if you use the rice cooker every day like me. To me, this is worth every penny, but may not be the right choice for everyone.

date: Reviewed in the United States on December 29, 2020
author: Tristan
title: Not just a rice cooker
body: Many of you might be wondering if there is any sense in buying $400~$500 rice cookers. Especially since rice costs so little and is usually not the main dish everyone looks forward to eating. It might make less sense and thus might be harder to justify the cost for the ones that don't eat rice regularly. However, for those that do eat rice on a regular basis, and especially for those that enjoy eating sticky short-grain rice instead of the dry long-grain ones you can find in American supermarkets, investing in a quality rice cooker makes much more sense.Even though rice is never the main dish on the table (with the exception for when you make rice dishes like fried rice, bibimbap, etc), having quality rice is still very important for elevating the overall dining experience. In my opinion, having quality rice is really important because of how it compliments so many other dishes (especially the Asian dishes). Imagine eating a really good pork belly kimchi stew but only having mediocre flaky rice to go with it instead of fresh steaming sticky rice cooked to perfection.Good rice cookers not only cook the rice perfectly but they also keep the rice fresh for a lot longer, preventing it from getting all mushy shortly after cooking it like they usually do with cheap rice cookers. This is convenient because then you don't have to cook rice every time you eat to have fresh rice; the 3 day old rice in these quality rice cookers will still taste fresh thanks to its induction heating technology.These are the two things I look for when I buy new rice cookers: Pressure Cooking & Induction Heating. Pressure cooking is how the rice cooker ensures your rice cooks perfectly and gets all gelatinous while keeping the cooking time super short. Induction heating is how the rice cooker keeps the cooked rice fresh for longer. Usually, rice cookers that have both features will run expensive, like this model does. In my opinion, the extra cost is worth it. Additionally, rice cookers that has both IH and Pressure Cooking are built with quality materials to give consumers the premium experience.Lastly, don't view these premium rice cookers as being simple kitchen appliances used for cooking rice and rice only. They have so many other ways they can be utilized!PS - You guys are probably confused as to why the content of my review doesn't match with the rating I'm giving this product. Well,  I knocked two stars off because I received a used product. I'm going to get it replaced but everything I said about this product still stands. Oh, and I also received a different color with the one I received being silver.

date: Reviewed in the United States on September 4, 2020
author: Tuckers Kahuna
title: Makes the best rice I've ever had.
body: The rice cooker I've been using started to burn the bottom of the rice, and I realized that I've had it for almost 25 years!  So I thought I'd treat myself to a super nice cooker this time, and I found this on sale. Maybe it will last another 25 years!The Cuckoo is a bit cuckoo to setup. It's very complicated and weird, but worth the effort. I love the quality of rice it makes, whether it's long grain Thai basmati, or short grain Japanese style, it comes out delicious.  I am using a water ratio of about 1.9x water to rice for most rice.  Short grain brown rice  also came out good with a bit more water added. I also tried the germinated brown rice recipe.  I gotta say though, the texture and taste of that was not much different than regular brown rice cooking, so not worth the extra effort for me.I like short grain 'kokari' style japanese rice the best, but with this cooker I've liked all types I have tried. I've seen reviews saying that Cuckoo doesn't do as well with long grain rice, but I LOVE the long grain rice I've made in it even more than short grain.  Even tho I'm not a big fan of brown rice, it's nearly as good as white in the Cuckoo so I will be eating more of it.  I have even tried a wild rice blend, and that was excellent.  I do tend to like stickier rice, probably more towards the Korean than Japanese style and the Cuckoo delivers.One of the reasons I got this cooker is the size, as a single person, I don't need a lot of rice.  And I typically use a 1/4 cup of dried rice to make a single portion.  This takes about 13 minutes on turbo mode or about 22 minutes on regular. So super quick and easy.  I have also found that larger portions take only a bit more time.  My experience with previous cookers is that doubling the size of the portion will usually double the time.  Not with the Cuckoo, it only adds 10-25% of the time.I was really skeptical of the auto-clean feature, but it actually works extremely well.  I clean out  the bowl, wipe down the upper lid and run it, and the whole unit comes out very clean.  I have made some recipes with a bit more ingredients and found that the insides do 'gum up' a bit more, and removing the top lid and cleaning it is essential.  Running the auto-clean twice made it sparkling after that.  My one irritation is that its hard to remove the top inner lid.  It's really clamped down and I have to work hard to get it off.  Could be a challenge for someone with arthritis or mobility issues.  Sometimes I've had to use a pair of pliers.The voice guide is essential because it's so complicated.  And I find the voice funny to hear.  It talks so much and unnecessarily.  It will announce it's going into sleep mode, for instance. Why would I need to know that?My only negatives for this cooker is the price, the size which is large for how small the portion of rice it makes, the weight, it's something like 12 lbs, so hard to move around.  And the difficulty in removing the inner lid, that is irritating.

date: Reviewed in the United States on August 25, 2019
author: Dino Sarma
title: 4 stars with reservations
body: The turbo mixed rice setting is perfect for Jasmine rice and other long grain rices. The glutinous rice regular setting works well for sushi rice, and the glutinous rice turbo setting works well for sona masoori and ponni rice. I have yet to try actual glutinous rice, but will update once I do. Basmati rice does well in the mixed rice turbo setting. Parboiled long grain rice works well in the glutinous rice regular setting. Parboiled basmati does well with the mixed rice turbo setting. The brown rice setting has worked well for brown basmati, brown long grain, brown Jasmine, and brown sushi rice. I do err on the side of adding slightly more water than the cooker calls for. If you forget to soak your brown rice overnight (it produces a softer brown rice which is less challenging to chew), you can use the GABA rice setting, and you'll get the same effect. It takes a couple hours, but it makes a brown rice that doesn't taste like health food.To access the turbo setting when you're making something, hit the start button twice. While the rice is cooking, there will be multiple times that the cooker will tell you it's doing something. Before it starts venting out the steam (it is a pressure cooker after all), it gives you a heads-up. If you don't like to use the microwave to reheat something, the multi cook function hit for 10 minutes will get your wet food (soup, stew, beans, etc) from fridge cold to nice and hot.What the superpower that this rice cooker has over larger models is sheer /speed/. I can wash off some rice, hit the glutinous rice turbo setting, and it'll be done /from start to finish/ in 12 - 16 minutes, depending on how much rice is in there. This isn't 12 minutes from when it hits pressure. This isn't 12 minutes from after it comes to a boil. This is where I hit start, and the cooker starts timing 12 minutes. When that timer has run out, the rice is ready, period. If you're impatient like me, add about 1/3 cup extra water to the mix, and you won't have to let the rice sit there and redistribute for 10 minutes after the cooking is done. The rice will be on the slightly softer side, but that's not entirely a bad thing, in my opinion. I don't care for rice that's too hard.The self cleaning function is where this guy really shines. There's been times where I'll cook something, and the top plate gets this starchy film all over it, and I don't want to spend all day cleaning it. The auto clean loosens up any stuck on food bits from the inner pot, and the lid, so that you can easily wipe it off.This isn't a small appliance though. Even though it's a 3 cup, the body is still fairly bulky. As counter top footprint goes, it's on par with my air fryer. That said, because I use it so much, it's worth it for me. I like the smaller size, because it keeps me from making too much of something, and having leftovers forever. I don't mind a leftover that I can clear off in one or two meals, but much more than that, and I'll get bored. I live alone, and it's easy to make too much of something, and then never want to eat it again, because you've had it twice a day five days in a row.

First of all there are few recipes internet-wide for this model. Secondly, despite being highly educated and many hours spent re-reading the manual??..it??s like a wispy cloud being pushed across the sky by the wind. In other words??..it??s beautiful but I can??t make heads or tails of the manual or even the measuring marks on the inside of the pot. I am disgruntled.Every bowl of rice I have made so far is over cooked or undercooked. I thought finally having an end all to end all GABA rice menu would be like dying and going to heaven. I used the high pressure and it turned out like mush. I used just the regular setting, having soaked the GABA overnight??.and it was hard and dry.Right now it??s sitting on the counter mocking me??unused, as I went back to using my Sakura and my Vita Clay rice cooker????unless you are a rocket scientist, or you can afford to waste extremely expensive high end Japanese rice, which I can??t, think carefully before purchasing.My Yum Yum Asia Sakura, as it turns out, is a really good, easy to use rice cooker??..My Vitaclay remains my favorite as I can watch over the rice cooking and stop cooking when it??s reached the perfect texture for whatever recipe I am making. Sometimes in life, you don??t really need the Cadillac.This costs a pretty penny??..the penny is a shiny bauble that is now a paperweight. It??s beautiful to look at, it??s made like a tank????but??..Update: returned. It doesn??t do anything a normal rice cooker or IP can??t. All these useless features like it talks to you? Nope.

date: Reviewed in the United States on June 6, 2024
author: D. Zebrowski
title: Always told an Asian rice maker is best, makes sense, and this agrees. Cuckoo!
body: I mean, surely in Asia they are among the chief consumers of rice as the center of their diet, where we here in the US tend more toward potatoes, primarily. I probably eat fries 5-10x more than I do rice, and that number is likely considerably higher.In truth, I think aside from convenience, one of the main reasons this is the case is that rice in the US, outside of an Asian restaurant, simply isn't very good. I mean, it's alright, not bad at all. Yet in truth, easily the best rice I ever eat is in a Japanese restaurant, Chinese following closely behind in my own culinary experiences.As such, I indeed had often heard that if I ever really wanted a proper rice maker, I would do best to purchase one actually manufactured and made for an Asian audience, even if it meant having to deal with foreign controls.Thankfully, with this premium rice maker and from a brand well known to be a rice cooking leader, they also included an option for English speech. Wow, so thankful! Kind of scary to get it to turn on, but with a few guesses, in the end, just push the buttons named in the fairly obscure manual, get it to selection 3, and English will greet you from then on.So, to the actual review.First, this rice maker is simply solid. Premium to be certain. I've used a few rice makers in recent years of varying quality, and this is hands down the best by a country mile. Landslide victory, not only in its build and controls, but most importantly, in the rice it produces.I consider is fairly obvious, but I'll take a moment to point it out anyway, that this is a pressure cooker, outright. It has a whole design around producing the best rice in quite a variety of iterations, but when it comes down to it, what makes it extra special is that it can make a premium quality batch of rice in about 15 minutes. Huge bonus points and bragging rights.Think of it as an instant pot, yet made specifically for rice, only (with a few exceptions, or additions. "Multi-cook" is the generic cook function on the device.The controls are a but daunting to learn, with all of the options. Yet I so thoroughly enjoy the Asian cultural norms, the bright and peppy messages, the built in humor. All quite fun and uplifting to me. Once you get your feet wet, it's actually quite simple, all in all.Perhaps one of the most important questions for me, as to whether or not this device would really see much counter time was the cleaning. Would it be easy, or a complicated process? Well, both primary ways for cleaning are so super simple.One, the bowl within is so well made, the surface so non-stick, that cleaning it after a batch takes almost no time at all by hand. The second way is to use the cleaning cycle, which is also super simple and easy. Drop some water to the mark, push the button, and 15 minutes or so later, all done. Sanitized.The first batch of rice was better than any I had made in years, and believe me, I have tried a ridiculous number of methods and means. It was easy, and super quick. White rise, turbo. Super sticky, yet not the least bit "glommy". Yum. Had such a good dish of Gen. Tsao chicken. Wonderful.So, in the end, if you like rice and want the best, or simply want to have a premier rice maker, this is easily an option for the top of the list, and I mean for rice makers world-wide.Ultimately, and according to wisdom and expectation, those who eat rice the most have also perfected the maker thereof. I am very thankful to have it, and look forward to all of my future rice dishes simply being terrific.

body: This is a powerful and advanced pressure cooker. date: Reviewed in the United States on May 29, 2024
author: Ree M??ndez
title: Induction Heating Pressure Rice Cooker
body: The CUCKOO CRP-JHR0609F is an exceptional 6-cup induction heating pressure rice cooker that brings both advanced technology and elegant design to your kitchen. This cooker, made in Korea, stands out with its sophisticated copper finish and robust functionality, making it a fantastic addition for any rice enthusiast.One of the standout features of this rice cooker is its 13 menu options. Whether you're cooking white rice, brown rice, GABA rice, porridge, or even making a quick cook, this versatile machine handles it all with precision. The induction heating technology ensures even cooking, resulting in perfectly cooked rice every time. The pressure cooking feature further enhances the texture and flavor, giving you restaurant-quality results at home.The auto-clean function is a huge time-saver, making maintenance a breeze. With just a touch of a button, the cooker cleans itself, ensuring hygiene and convenience. Additionally, the voice guide feature is particularly helpful, especially for those new to using advanced rice cookers. It provides clear instructions and feedback, available in multiple languages, making the cooking process straightforward and user-friendly.The build quality of the CUCKOO CRP-JHR0609F is impressive, reflecting the high standards of Korean manufacturing. The materials used are durable and the design is both modern and aesthetically pleasing, fitting seamlessly into any kitchen decor.In terms of capacity, the 6-cup (uncooked) size is perfect for small to medium-sized families, offering ample servings without taking up excessive counter space. The intuitive control panel and clear display further enhance the overall user experience, making this rice cooker easy to operate even for beginners.Overall, the CUCKOO CRP-JHR0609F rice cooker is a high-performing, reliable, and stylish kitchen appliance. Its advanced features, ease of use, and excellent cooking results make it well worth the investment for anyone serious about their rice.

date: Reviewed in the United States on May 27, 2024
author: DS
title: One of the best household rice cookers on earth
body: This is my second Cuckoo induction rice cooker.  Almost everyone in my family has either a Cuckoo or Cuchen.  I still use my original cooker which is a 3 cup for when I'm feeding just myself or one other, but I'll use this 6 cup cooker for feeding a group of 4-6 people.  The induction way of cooking rice has always given me perfect rice every time with no burnt sides.  But don't worry, you can still make nooroongji with this cooker.  There are other options for using it to make soups, brown rice, etc. as  well.  I really just use it for tasty plain white rice.  The self-cleaning feature works nicely, though you'll still want to do a deep clean when it gets too dirty.  I love the voice prompts/responses.

date: Reviewed in the United States on May 25, 2024
author: JoeB
title: One of my best kitchen appliances
body: This is one of the best kitchen items that I now have.  It makes perfect sticky white rice just like the restaurants.  So far have only tested with white rice as that I all I had.  Will be getting other rices and enjoying them in this cooker as well.  Everything is marked just add the number of cups of rice using the enclosed rice measuring cup and add water to the matching cup line.  Then close and lock the lid and select the type of rice being cooked.  Everything is automated and it voices what is happening during the cooking process.  The most difficult item for me was getting it set to English not knowing what the directions meant by "Standby mode".  Standby mode is when all the indicators go out when sitting a long time.  The 3 language options are Korean, English, and Chinese. with the default option being Korean.Cleanup is easy with this cooker as all the items needing washed are easily removed for cleaning and it has a setting for sterilization cleaning.  Will also be trying some of the slow-cook meal options.

date: Reviewed in the United States on May 24, 2024
author: Charles P.
title: Fast shipping
body: I like that this cooker cooks rice perfectly and quickly. I also like that the inner lid gasket. inner lid and steam valve can all be easily taken apart for cleaning. There is a metal poker attached to the bottom of the cooker to use if the stream valve gets clogged. The rice pot itself is heavy duty and well constructed. The voice language is easy to change to English, Korean or Chinese. For me the rice took 28 minutes as this cooker cooks under pressure and releases the pressure just prior to the cooking process finishing. 28 minutes is a full 10 minutes faster than the old rice cooker this one replaced and the rice tastes better. Overall as a daily use kitchen appliance this CUCKOO rice cooker will more than pay for itself.

date: Reviewed in the United States on May 23, 2024
author: AnonyAr
title: I love rice!
body: I'm one of those people that grew up with a rice cooker, but was never taught how it could be used it for more than just rice, it was just known and never acknowledged. Growing up, they were all pretty basic, to middle of the road, type of rice cookers, most of which have the electric plate heating and a variety of different types of stainless steel, aluminum, or non-stick coated pans.I've always wanted one of those fancy rice cookers that talks, has all kinds of sound effects, music  and/or talks to you, and I grabbed this mostly because I wanted to see if there really is a big difference between the ones I've mentioned vs one that costs 3-5x more than some rice cookers, and has induction, and/or pressure cooking features.Straight out of the box, it has this beautiful, brushed copper finish, black and silver details all around. On top of that it has backlit wording, a inset lit screen for the time notifications, lock and unlock dial, a pressure release valve and more. I greatly appreciate the fact that the inside plate on the top, as well as the pressure valve casing comes off, and there is a water dispenser on the back side , for easy cleaning. The cooking pot/bowl has sturdy plastic handles, which are easy to the touch when the rest of the pot is hot. The inside of the bowl is coated and has all kinds of indication lines for anything you can think of. Seriously, this thing is a tank and it's super bougie. Like so fancy, with so many details and features, I feel like I shouldn't have it in my possession.My first dish would naturally be rice, to get a good baseline, but I decided to go hard and made Filipino porridge called Lugaw, which I normally cook on the stove in under an hour. I checked the recipe in the given manual, to get an idea of ratios needed and used that mostly as a guide since lugaw contains some other ingredients than a standard chicken porridge, plus I wanted to make double the amount. The measuring cup included, doesn't have ccs noted for liquid measure, so I had to do a quick conversion of 1.23cups, that equates to 300cc of water/broth. Thankfully the rice bowl also has liquid lines noted inside too, but I still felt like I had a little bit less water than I should've used. Either way, I set it all and forgot it. About an hour later, it sounded off like a choo choo, indicating it was releasing steam and soon let me know it was time. I opened up the rice cooker, and it was magic. I mixed everything around, everything was evenly cooked, and nothing was burned one bit! Did I need more liquid? Not really, the rice cooker just knew. I actually like my lugaw thicker, but could easily add more broth to make it a little lighter, thinner, and soupy.Honestly, I've looked high and low, for videos and data on induction cooking rice makers like this and the reasons why this costs that much and I'm still trying to figure that out, as every video I saw gave the basics, and those on the tubes just made rice with no clear data, or idea on how to review a rice cooker. I'm not saying this will be #1 review, but I'm trying. The big things the company advertises is that this one provides a higher pressure than most, and it has induction heating, which ensures more even cooking. My mother is convinced that it eliminates starch, but that's a high claim and I'm not a believer in that so much, with any type of rice cooker. The options and guidance are definitely a big plus with this, and I plan on making it my right hand man in the kitchen next to the air fryer, since time and energy is so limited when it comes to making meals up to 2-3 times a day. On top of that, it makes just enough food for 2 people, and has some leftover for the next day compared to my slow cooker that can take all day, and up to 8 hours for one meal, and more leftovers than my freezer can handle!Honestly, this guy is not necessary if you plan on using a rice cooker like this JUST to make rice, but I have seen lower level models with the same, or similar features from this company at big box warehouses. This is really the bougie version of certain models with all the fancy details, mechanics, and look. Plus it's a beast compared to my other 6 cup rice cooker, so you'll also have to accommodate for the size in your kitchen. Think deep, not wide. Either way, I'm totally keeping this since I plan to make it more of an everyday appliance, compared to my other rice cooker that just takes a nap in the cupboard and comes out to play once a week. Try it if you must, but know if you pay the price, appliances like these are more of an investment.

date: Reviewed in the United States on May 21, 2024
author: Cheryl
title: The manual is confusing, disorganized and has tiny print but it makes great congee!
body: The white rice is clumpy despite rinsing it until completely clear.  The brown rice was best but I realized I used the wrong water line and used less water.I also made Gaba rice which turned out good, it was slightly clumpy although the texture was good after adding butter.  After some googling, I found out that after you soak the rice for 16 hours, the Gaba process sprouts the rice during a 2-hour process and the last hour cooks the rice.But even the Gaba rice was slightly clumpy.  I did let the rice rest for 10 mins this time and it was better.I had to start highlighting sections of the manual so I could find the scattered information.  Page 17 is very important because it is there that it tells you the water line to use for Brown Rice.  There is a small tidbit about the Direct Touch menus but there is no title to grab your attention and the print is so tiny it??s easy to miss.  There is no mention of the steam plate.To start, there is a MODE button and a Selection dial and the voice helps you along, thankfully.  When you turn her on, she is speaking Korean, so I was in a bit of a panic but it was easy to use the Mode function to set to English AND to set the time.I focused on cooking rice and ignored setting up timers for reheating and warming, and the My Mode feature, until I am comfortable with the settings.I tested how well it cooks rice by making medium grain brown rice.  Each grain was perfectly cooked despite using less water.  Then I tried white jasmine rice, which turned out very clumpy, even though I had rinsed it until the water was super clear.I wanted to use the steamer plate.  No where in the manual does it say anything about steaming.  There??s a Multi-Cook function but the only thing it mentions is how to change the preset time of the Multi-Cook function. I contacted support for this reason and they sent me a PDF manual that has a different recipe table than what is in the manual that came with my machine.  So again, the manual is lacking.The 6-cup unit is a little over 14 inches in depth but the feet sit approximately 10 inches apart.  It??s not terribly heavy, so easy to move around.  The cord is located on the left side and fairly short.  The cup markings inside are white which makes them easy to see, which I love. One side is Korean, the other side is printed in English.There is no way to turn off the unit, which bugs me but it does turn to power save modeTo make rice on any of the Direct Touch selections, merely add rice and then add water to the correct line and then touch your selection.  Brown rice you must also use the Gaba line. I??m a bit disappointed as I make better rice on the stove-top.  I am still researching to see if I'm missing something.

date: Reviewed in the United States on May 21, 2024
author: Iphone user
title: A significant improvement over older rice cookers
body: My family eats rice at least 5 days a week and we are loving this rice cooker.  We came from a 10 year old Zojirushi (non-induction) which is already a good rice cooker however I was really surprised at the improvements this model offered.The best improvement is that the pressure cooking makes the rice firm and fluffy at the same time.  With the right amount of water dialed in, the rice has a wonderful texture and mouth feel.Second since it uses pressure, the cooking time is drastically reduced.  A 45minute wait is now down to 26 minutes for 3 servings!  The machine can tell how much rice is inside and displays an estimate as soon as you press the cook button.  It also talks to you in English, Korean or Chinese.We have made long grain jasmine and calrose rice with this and both produce rice that is a significant improvement over our old rice cooker.There are all sorts of presets for mixed rice, brown rice, and steamer and porridge functions which we rarely use but it's there.  There is also a customized rice cooking program where you can dial in exactly how you want the rice to turn out.The price of this rice cooker is significant.. however for an appliance which will be used almost everyday, I believe it is well worth the time savings and most importantly, the improvement in texture you will get.

body: This is hands down the best rice cooker I??ve ever used! I previously had another brand??s IH & pressure rice cooker that was also expensive, but I never felt it was significantly better than the less pricey ones. So I was hesitant to spend this much again, but it turned out that this one is definitely worth the price! I??ve found myself eating way more rice than before because it??s just that delicious.That said, the UI could use some improvement. The interface is overly complicated compared to other brands, and the buttons have very small text and require a firm press to work. While I can adapt, it??s not user-friendly, especially for elderly users like my mom. She initially wanted to return it because of these issues, but the quality of the rice won her over.If the UI were simpler and more intuitive, this would be perfect. Still, highly recommend it for anyone who prioritizes amazing rice quality!

body: I may tested with two small batches (1/2 of the provided cup) on high pressure (26 mins) and turbo (12 mins) but both resulted in quite thick crust at the bottom. The cooked rice was a bit too sticky to my taste. The deal breakers for me are lot of steam and very loud ~70 dB as I measured and higher than 80dB when release the pressure. I could even hear from upstair shower. I really like the pot with handles though. I eat rice all my rice and from one of the biggest rice export country so not unfamiliar with rice cookers. I did not want to waste more rice to test with bigger batch so hard to say if it would perform like other reviews.

date: Reviewed in the United States on April 23, 2024
author: Haowen Wang
title: Low Quality Rice Cooker and Poor Customer Service
body: **Title: Disappointing Durability and Poor Customer Service****Rating: 1 out of 5 stars**I was initially attracted to the sleek design of the Cuckoo rice cooker and had high hopes for it. Unfortunately, just a few days into using it, I noticed cracks developing on the lid, which was both surprising and disappointing given its reputation and price point. When I reached out for support, the experience went downhill. Amazon's return window had just closed, being slightly over a month, and they were unwilling to offer a replacement. Turning to Cuckoo's customer service proved equally frustrating; they simply refused to address the issue, leaving me stuck with a defective product. Overall, this purchase was a letdown due to the poor build quality and the lack of responsible customer service from both Amazon and Cuckoo. Buyers, be wary and consider other options!

date: Reviewed in the United States on March 27, 2024
author: MacGuffin
title: Great Little Cooker
body: I'm a fan of Cuckoo products and own a multi cooker and 10-cup rice cooker that's the "papa bear" equivalent of this "baby bear." This twin pressure induction cooker is Cuckoo's top-of-the-line three-cup model and it's a gem.Like my other Cuckoo products, this is a high-quality appliance. The inner pot is very heavy and has a premium, and very attractive non-stick surface. What's nice is that the various types of rice can either be cooked under pressure or steamed, depending on how you like your rice. You can also cook it super-fast with the turbo setting, in addition to other settings for things like baby food and stored (i.e. frozen) rice.GABA is the setting for plain, pre-germinated, or cooker-germinated brown rice. It's necessary, if the latter is your choice, to choose the 3-hour setting; the other two settings are pretty much press-and-play. The cycle takes quite a while from start to finish--just shy of four hours. However, it's a much less fussy way of germinating rice than worrying about pre-soaking, and the nutritional values are pretty much the same either way. If you come home for lunch, you can start your GABA rice before you return to work and it'll be ready for dinner. Even if you opt not to use the four-hour cycle, your rice will stay warm until you return, courtesy of the "stay warm" setting that kicks in automatically when cooking is completed.I wish this unit had a "scorched" setting and, unfortunately, the cooked cereal settings are gone as well. It's tiny and, I suppose, can only accommodate so many functions but cooked cereals would've been great for a single person. I also wish its steam plate was like that of its "papa's," i.e. with a push button instead of having to pull it off (like my multi cooker's). It's also something of a pain to place its "packing," i.e. sealing ring, after you've cleaned it. I discovered that the best way is to place it completely as best you can before attempting to secure it. Make sure to align its tabs with the cover's holes.The instruction booklet is rather opaque--you can tell that whoever translated it from Korean wasn't completely comfortable with English. Supplemental literature that explains the various functions, along with some recipe suggestions, wouldn't have come amiss, especially at this cooker's price point. We can only hope that someone at Cuckoo actually reads these reviews.All in all, I'm extremely pleased with this little guy. I have a large house but a very small kitchen and this saves me from having to pull out the multi cooker if I'm just preparing rice (the 10-cup is only for guests or unless I'm really jonesing for scorched rice). I picked it up on sale, plus there was a $10 coupon. If you need a rice cooker that's less capacious than six cups, this is the one to get.

date: Reviewed in the United States on February 18, 2024
author: Simon
title: Defective pressure cooking function
body: This product is defective.  For a price tag of $656, it only works for cooking rice.  The reason we bought this is also for its pressure-cooking function.  When it worked (only a couple of times since we bought it), food come out pretty good, of course not as good as the true pressure cooker on the market.  However, after using the pressure-cooking function for about a couple times, it started to leak steams the top part.  We didn't know that it was actually leaking as we thought it's just normal pressure release.  But most of the meat recipe that we cooked with pressure cooking function, all of them turned out uncooked for the meat.  For whole grain, they come out to be under cooked, extremely dry, and sometime burnt due to leaking steam.Apparently, this is a very common problem for this cooker.  There are quite a bit of complaints about such problem and there are many Youtube clips showing how to fix them.  But it requires taking the cooker apart.  With a new cooker, if we were to take it apart would void the warrantee.  However, we have reached out to Cuckoo for support.  They only response once asking about where it was purchased.  After presenting the order information from Amazon, they went dead silent, and it's been months now.To the potential buyers, beware, when you make this expensive decision to purchase this product.  It's defective and support is virtually nonexistent.  Hope you have better luck if you decide to purchase one.To Cuckoo, I hope you are reading this.  Please make better product as your price point is very high.  Have some decency to support your customers as you are leaving them a bad product and experience.

body: We purchased this cooker when the price dropped a bit. So I would say the value for the money is good. The design is very modern and pretty. I love the cute sounds it makes with each stage of cooking. It is also easy to clean.  We had been using an Insta Pot to cook rice. The rice came out okay with the Insta, but the warm function would always burn or dry out the rice. The Cuckoo not only seems to cook the rice quicker, but it comes out perfect! I have tried both high pressure and non pressure modes. Both come out perfect. High pressure makes the rice seem a bit stickier but really good. Non pressure rice is drier so it is better for fried rice type dishes. Keep warm on the Cuckoo actually keeps the rice warm without drying it out or burning. I haven??t tried the other functions yet. We bought it solely for cooking rice and it is not disappointing.

date: Reviewed in the United States on June 15, 2023
author: Pamela
title: Good while it worked
body: I purchased 2 of these rice cookers, 1 for my mother and 1 for my family. The device worked well while it worked. We only ever used it for rice and it did a good job at cooking rice. It is much heavier and bigger than our previous rice cookers. Unfortunately, both our devices lasted almost 2 years before they started failed.For my mother's, the inner pot where you cook the rice started to peel. The peeling got so bad it was unusable. This started happening after the warranty, 1 year. The solution - to buy a new inner pot for $89.99 on the Cuckoo site.For my device, the 'ECO' message appeared and the device stopped working. This happened earlier this week after the warranty period. I contacted Cuckoo and they advised that I could send it to their California location to be fixed but I am responsible for shipping and repair costs. I looked up how much it would cost me to ship the device and the cheapest was UPS Ground for about $65. They gave me an estimate that it would be $30 shipping + $40 repair. The total would be $135.00.For such an expensive device, it should have lasted longer. I'm not sure if I will repair it or buy another from this brand. In the past my family had Zojirushi rice cookers and they lasted longer than 2 years. In fact, I pulled out my old one to use when the Cuckoo stopped working.This is a nice device and it worked well when it worked. If you don't mind buying a new one every few years and the price is not an issue then consider this rice cooker. If you'd like something that lasts longer, is priced less, and don't want to have a purchase a new one every few years, then it would be better to consider another rice cooker.

date: Reviewed in the United States on April 27, 2021
author: MH
title: Bad Build Quality: Fit and Finish Raise Questions about Durability
body: This Cuckoo rice cooker does not feel like a $500 rice cooker. There's something about all of the mechanical parts that just feels like it's going to break at any moment. From the front panel, to the lid release button, to the way the lid swings open and clangs the bowl -- it's all very cheap feeling. Even my Instant Pot feels better quality. The Cuckoo looks nice, but once you touch it, it's plasticy and unrefined, and just doesn't match what I expected from this price range. In my Asian American household, where rice is made almost every day, there is no way I could trust this to be my kitchen workhorse and stand up to years of use. Sorry Cuckoo -- I was hoping your cool designs would be paired with higher build quality.It's back to the Japanese brands for us. I've used a Zojirushi IH for 10+ years that still works great, even if it's getting a little dinged up. My mom's Zojirushi is probably pushing 20 years. Everything about Zojirushi rice cookers feels much higher quality than this Cuckoo. The Zoji's aesthetics are not great, but at least I can trust it will do its job for years to come.

author: Gentai Ni
title: Manual's English Translation Missing Content
body: If you flip through the manual, you'll notice the English and Mandarin translations are missing entire pages of information compared to the Korean part, such as cleaning/care instructions (!) and setting voice volume/other menu functions. Even with the voice mode set to English, it's not apparent what some of the settings do when you adjust them. It's a glaring omission by Cuckoo and needs to be fixed.

date: Reviewed in the United States on April 20, 2021
author: Angry Dad
title: Quality control issues (Amazon Warehouse Mishandling)
body: As a korean, I really wanted to like this rice cooker but it's absolute garbage. I had to get a replacement because the cooker verbally told us it's broken and to call customer service.. on first use. I got a replacement and this one has skid marks that I can't remove. I'd expect a $600 rice cooker to come in prestine condition.UPDATE: THE REPLACEMENT UNIT IS ALSO FAULTY. The rice cooker tells us to call customer service. (See error message pic)UPDATE 2: Third time's a charm? I bought the same thing from a local korean market. Why? Had a feeling. Ended up working like a champ. These rice cookers have a lot of electrical components to it and is pretty sensitive to mishandling. I wouldn't be surprised if careless amazon delivery caused the internal damages. Updated to 3 stars from 1.UPDATE 3: I've been using this rice cooker everyday nonstop 24/7 for the past 6 months. It still holds up like a champ and I expect it to do so for at least the next 10 years. Best rice cooker (UPDATE 4: After 3 years of daily use, the faulty error came back. Our local Korean market vendor took it back to cuckoo's LA location to get it repaired for ~$30. Y'all should be buying this from a rice cooker monger in a Korean supermarket, if you can. It breaks a lot.

author: RCH
title: This is a great Rice Cooker for the money
body: I own two of the Zojirushi Commercial rice cookers but needed more production capability (cooked rice).  I was relooking my options (the Zo is more than $400 for the 20 cup commercial).  I found this Cuckoo model and did a bit of research.  The Cuckoo brand is Korean and is considered to be a top of the line cooker.Pluses:  Half the cost of the Zo Commercial cooker.  50% more production capacity than the Zo  (30 cup vs 20 cup) per cycle.I bought one of these and used it for about 10 days (we do a 50 pound bag of rice every morning).  Both Zo's plus the new Cuckoo helped.  After a week, I ordered a second of these Cuckoos.  (both of these together cost less than a third zo).I really now prefer the Cuckoos to the Zo's.  For us, the capacity of the Cuckoo is perfect (max volume before the lift from the pot becomes too heavy).  The fact that there is a third indicator light (shows cooking, steaming, and warming) rather that the two for the Zo (cook and warm) so you don't have to time your warming light to estimate steaming time.  When the Cuckoo leaves steam and goes to warm, the rice is ready.I can't speak to longevity yet because the short time I have owned them.  I can say they make perfect jasmine, basmati, and sushi style rice, and do it well.v/r rh

author: hp
title: Great rice cooker
body: This rice cooker is great. Got this to cook large quantities of rice and this does not disappoint. The outside is all stainless steel, which is great for a commercial kitchen. It's easy to clean the outside. The inside is non-stick and the main container is was to clean. The included rubber bottom helps keep the rice from sticking and it's easy to clean off. The measuring cup included is perfect so you can get exactly the amount you need. Cooking the rice takes a while depending on the quantity, but not longer than expected. It's a great cooker for a commercial kitchen or if you have a need to cook very large quantities. I would definitely recommend it.

date: Reviewed in the United States on April 15, 2024
author: Adasterix
title: Straight forward high yield rice cooker
body: From the product description, this rice cooker is intended for making large batches of rice typical for events or scenarios where dozens of people are being served.  It is not quite up to full kitchen duty for a professional restaurant where operation would be needed non-stop during the day, but for daily use at a bed and breakfast or hospitality service or banquet this cooker can really shine.The build quality of this cooker is quite good.  The lid latch is well designed and secures reliably.  The metal casing has a nice brushed steel finish.  The inner cooking pot has a good quality non-stick coating and the pot itself feels sturdy and durable.  The included plastic spoon is durable and does a great job at grabbing cooked rice.  The included silicon ring mat does a great job at preventing rice from overcooking and fusing to the bottom of the pot.  The bottom feet are plastic, and do not have rubber soles, however due to the size of this cooker I did not experience an issue where it slid around on my countertop.Operation of this rice cooker is simple, measure rice and water according to the instructions included with the cooker, then press down the cook latch to start the process which from there is fully automated. No additional steps are needed. The cooker has measure marks embossed on the pot portion to help make these measurements quickly. They are marked at 10, 20, and 30 cup ratios with matching marks on where to fill water.  A simple ratio that seems to work if being more precise is 1.5 cups water for ever 1 cup rice.  I tried this on a small batch of 2 cups rice to 3 cups water (4 servings) and this cooker did really well.  It will automatically bring the water to boiling temperature then switch to steam mode where excess moisture is let off through a steam vent at the top.  Additional excess moisture is also captured in a small removable clear plastic bin near the hinge of the cooker as needed.  For this small batch, this overflow was not needed.  The resulting rice quality was perfect.  I did have a bit that stuck to the silicone mat, which should be removed as soon as cooking as finished.  This is likely due to not washing the rice quite as much as was needed, as starches can "glue" the rice down if not washed thoroughly.  But overall, I am very pleased with the results even in smaller batches and look forward to using this for large events (or cooking several weeks worth of rice for home meals).  This is a great product that is simple to use and clean.  It is a little large for typical home use but can still work if there is space set aside for it and if rice is a staple of the family diet.

date: Reviewed in the United States on October 26, 2024
author: Luckyin
title: Compact and sleek, but slow
body: I got this coffee maker knowing that Cuckoo is a highly reputed kitchen appliance brand (i.e. rice cookers) and was intrigued at their foray into coffee.  I'm a novice coffee snob, but can appreciate the compromise in convenience vs. quality in K-cups.  Here are my thoughts after initial use:Pros:- Compact & sleek: It is very lightweight and occupies very little countertop real estate. It looks very nice and would fit in any modern kitchen.- It comes with two adapters: One for regular single-use K-cups, and a reusable K-cup adapter to add your own grinds.  This was important for me as I have a grinder and wanted to reduce waste from plastic K-cups.- Very easy interface: Just one button to cycle through cup size (with highly customizable volumes of 6, 8, 10, 12, and 14oz), and another button to brew.  No fancy touch-screens that are failure-prone.- Removable water basin in the back: This allows very easy filling as I can bring the basin directly to my sink and reattach it to the machine without having to lug an additional pitcher around to refill.- Regular Kcups seem to taste pretty consistent with other Keurig machinesCons:- Time to brew: This was a big one for me, as one of the biggest advantages of Keurig machines nowadays are how quickly they can brew.  An 8oz cup takes 175 seconds (per the manual).  That's a 3-minute brew cycle, which is much longer than other machines I've used.  If you want a bigger cup, the 12oz and 14oz brews take 220 seconds and 245 seconds, respectively.  Granted, this device is smaller than others that I've used, so the heating element is likely not as efficient, but definitely worth noting.- Resuable Kcup flavor is weak: I have my own burr grinder that I used to fill the resuable K-cup adapter to the max fill-line.  Despite the maximum addition of ~12g of fresh coffee grinds, the 10oz brew was still quite weak.  I may need to experiment with grind size and quantity, but overall the depth of flavor was lacking.  This does seem par for the course for reusable Kcups though, so I don't think this product is inferior to anything else on the market, but obviously cannot compare to a traditional drip coffee machine.  The metal mesh strainer also does let some grinds through, so I was left with a few grinds at the bottom of my cup.  Perhaps a single-use paper filter may fix this, but then adds to the complexity/waste as well.- I can't fit my travel mug under the machine to brew directly into it: I need to brew into a regular mug, then pour into my travel mug.All in all, this product is great for what it is: a sleek, low-footprint Kcup maker that is simple to use.  It's an elegant addition to any compact kitchen that seeks to conserve counter space.  However, the small size does compromise brew speed.  Great anyone looking for a basic cup of coffee.

author: Mike
title: Perfect brewer in both form and function
body: I was familiar with Cuckoo primarily for their sophisticated rice cookers. Its a brand I appreciated, so when I learned they offered a K-cup style coffee maker, I had to give it a shot.I've brewed a cup a coffee nearly everyday from this unit for the past 8 months. I've tried several brands of K-cups and used freshly grounded coffee on occasions. For a majority of these brews, I was provided a consistent temperature and quality pour. The flavors have been spot on with not much to complain about.This Cuckoo brewer has a super simple interface There are only couple of capitative buttons on the front display to control the volume of your pour and enable brew mode. Once you press start, it'll take less than 5 minutes for the water to heat and eventually pour your coffee. When brewing, its not very loud. Maybe a little quieter than an old school coffee maker.I appreciate the design of this unit. Its dimensions fit really well in my coffee corner. The water container is easy to remove too for clearing and its super easy to fill it up.Overall, I'm happy with this purchase and haven't came across any issues after 8 months. I've tried a few Krieg models before, and I find this Cuckoo coffee maker easier to use and just as good at brewing.

author: M. Hill
title: CUCKOO Single K-Cup Brewer with Loose Grounds Adapter
body: A compact simple k-cup brewer this unit also includes an adapter for use with the user‚Äôs ground coffee or loose tea. The machine brews slowly at approximately 3 minutes per cup although the machine I was using prior to this was also very slow and while initially annoying one quickly adapts by starting the process and doing some boring task while waiting. Clearly written the User Manual is easy to follow and operation is straightforward.The water tank holds 40 ounces of water so refilling is required less frequently. The adapter that holds the loose ground coffee with the recommended quantity being 1 tablespoon coffee for each 5 ounces of water but as I‚Äôve found with other similar adapters it is difficult to get a sufficiently strong cup of coffee. I like the idea of the grounds adaptor and have found using a dark espresso roast works best.Dark roast coffee is helpful but finding a satisfactory quantity to strength of the finished cup will require trial and error adjusted to suit individual preferences. Cup size selections are 6, 8, 10, 12 and 14 ounce cups. I brewed both a prepared k-cup capsule along with coffee grounds in the other adapter and both worked well. The booklet includes cleaning instructions and troubleshooting. Good basic k-cup brewer.\

date: Reviewed in the United States on March 24, 2025
author: Bryan
title: Clock has military time only; not disclosed in product page or instructions
body: The clock on this unit only runs in military time - I.E., when it's 10:30 PM, the clock reads "22:30" instead of 10:30 PM. There is no way to change this, and this quirk seems to not be disclosed in the product description or the instructions. I like all of my clocks to run on 12-hour time, especially in the kitchen. I have a Keurig machine that can do this - not sure why this machine can't.Additionally, there is some blue plastic hanging out of a crease between the control panel and the silver trim on the front of the unit that I couldn't pull away. It seems to be stuck to the inside of the unit somewhere and had escaped through the crease, but was anchored to whatever is behind the crease so there's no way to fully pull it away and it will always be visible as a blue interruption to an otherwise silver and black facade.Cuckoo needs to think through these better, and do better QC on the final products, before they start distributing.

body: I know the Cuckoo brand best from their excellent line of very durable rice makers, so I jumped at the chance to try out their coffee maker.First impressions: It is a very clean modern design, black with brushed stainless-steel wrap-around accents, 10-Cup capacity and a permanent cone filter basket. It is slightly smaller than our old one, which is great because it fits much better under our upper cabinets. It has a clock, two brew cycles [regular and strong], and some programming options - it is all very simple [not gimmicky or complex]. Our first cup of coffee was delicious. My wife especially likes the carafe's handle - says it really fits her hand very well.We are happy with our new Cuckoo coffee maker. We like its design - it has attractive clean lines and subtle character, plus it sits well on our worktop. And best of all, it makes a really nice cup of coffee!

author: Alan H. Weiner
title: Clock does not keep accurate time!
body: It makes coffee. How great is that? However the clock sucks and the reuseable filter, which is cone shaped is slightly smaller than the gold tone filter I have from my old coffee maker, and I do not see where you can get a repalcement if the one provided needs to be replaced. The clock problem is NOT that it is a 24 hour clock. The problem is that it keeps skipping ahead. I set it at night and in the morning it is running two or three hours fast. Since I do not use the program function, it is just a small problem, but if you want to program a start time, good luck! If you want a cheap coffee maker, this one is OK (so far, I have had it for about three weeks). If you want to program a start time, I would suggest spending more money and get one that keeps correct time. 10/10/25 Update. The filter funnel lost its washer. Cuckoo has no parts for replacement. They want $10 shipping for a new funnel. Be aware that the warranty does not cover parts (per Cuckoo). I wonder what the warranty actually covers? Will not buy their products again.

date: Reviewed in the United States on April 30, 2023
author: neelordeb
title: Works perfectly with homes that have sunlight levels to activate it.
body: This review is for the CUCKOO Air Purifier with 5-Stage H13 True HEPA Filter for Large-Sized (470 sq. ft.) Rooms, delivered on time and in perfect condition from Amazon.This Air Purifier works well when it works. It has an automatic sleep mode, when the daylight sensor shows it getting dark, it automatically goes into sleep mode, cutting off activity lights and going into a slower mode to allow for easier sleep.The button to put it in sleep mode is absolutely sufficient to put this in sleep when needed, the Auto light sensor is nothing but problematic at best ..My house is situated north to south (front to back) and as the sun tracks it does not actually shine through the windows, but indirectly lights the inside of my house. This sensor always is activated so the only option for me is to set to medium speed and let it run all the time in order to have this make a difference in my home - thus cancelling the auto, energy savings mode. This feature should not exist, or be set to actual dark situations - not activate when just dim. I am perfectly able to set the sleep mode manually.

date: Reviewed in the United States on April 15, 2023
author: Middle Path
title: Mixed review - filters great but has some issues
body: UPDATE:TL/DR: Filters great, but needs natural daylight to function as it should. Otherwise, it's in sleep mode, which the manual says you have to put it in, but mine goes to sleep mode when it's not bright enough. This means it only senses air quality and filters accordingly from about 7:30 am to 6 pm. Huge disappointment. Also, when the air is the worst quality like when I sear a steak and the whole place is cloudy with beef vapor, it doesn't turn on the TURBO mode and therefore doesn't make much of a dent in the vapor. I think it goes to medium or high. When I manually turn it to turbo it's great, but otherwise leaves a lot to be desired. Downgrading to 2 stars. I also stopped moving it into the bedroom to sleep because what's the point?I did not look at its size prior to pressing the "order" button and it's about 5x larger than I anticipated! The photo appeared to me like another desktop model I have so I was shocked when the waist-high box showed up at the door of the temporary rental I'm in for another 6 weeks at most. When I pulled The Beast out, I thought at least it looks kinda pleasant, let's see how to make this thing filter.PROS:It's reduced allergic reaction over the last two days. Was having sneezing fits in the morning and lots of drippy nose, even a little minor nosebleed when I woke up a few days ago, adjusting to a new desert climate. Yesterday (after first night of use) I had ZERO allergies, didn't even use one tissue or sneeze. Today I've had a sneeze or two, needed to use 2 tissues so far. I've been moving it into the bedroom while I sleep, and back out into the main living area during the day and it's not hard to move around using the convenient handles.CONS:Firstly, I thought I received a broken unit. It was dusk while I first tried it out, and I noticed the air-quality light would turn off after a minute. Kept pushing the button, timing how long it stayed on, etc. I read the manual back and forth, it said when you put it on sleep mode the light goes off, which made me think it would be ON at all other times when you did not ask it to go to sleep. Next morning in the daylight the light detector stayed on. So I guess it sleeps automatically? I can't tell if it's detecting and changing speed of filtration accordingly during this lack-of-daylight-auto-sleep mode.It also provides an extra (?) allergen filter, but there's nothing in the manual about how to use it. I didn't know if there's one already in use out of the box, or am I supposed to attach it? Is this a replacement filter? And if so, why are there instructions how to change or clean every other part of the filter and nothing in the manual about the allergen filter? Even the photo of the filters in the manual doesn't look anything like the gossamer sheet of allergen filter in a bag provided. There are instructions for how to replace the pre-filter, and how to replace the air purifying filter but NOTHING about the allergen filter other than it needs to be changed every 4 months. Would appreciate if they can post a video how to change it because it's unclear and I've spent a lot of time going back and forth through the manual unsuccessfully.

date: Reviewed in the United States on March 19, 2023
author: k0311
title: Silent and Efficient Air Purifier.
body: I recently purchased the Cuckoo air purifier and I have been extremely impressed with its performance. This air purifier deserves a solid 5-star rating in my book! The first thing that really impressed me was how silent it was when it was operating in smart mode. I could hardly even tell that it was on, which was a welcome relief compared to my previous air purifier which was quite noisy.â €Another great feature is its ability to detect dust and odor. It's amazing how quickly it picks up on any changes in the air quality. For example, I noticed that when I changed my bed sheets or put on some perfume from the master bathroom, the air purifier in my bedroom immediately detected the change and started working to filter out any dust and odors. It was quite impressive to see how sensitive it was to even the slightest changes in the air.â €Overall, I am extremely satisfied with the Cuckoo air purifier and would highly recommend it to anyone who wants a high-quality air purifier that is both effective and efficient. It is definitely worth the investment and has made a noticeable difference in the air quality in my home.

date: Reviewed in the United States on March 29, 2023
author: TT & The Bears & The Lion & The Witch & The Wardrobe
title: A very nice air purifier for larger rooms
body: Recently I've been noticing an increasing amount of cigarette smoke seeping into my apartment from the neighbors. The air purifier really moves a good amount of air and purifies the room fairly effectively. While this air purifier has a 5-stage HEPA filter, some odor-causing particles are smaller than they could filter. The nice thing about this air purifier is that it has a fine particle sensor (smaller than 2.5) so it will adjust its pollution sensor much more quickly and accurately. I have an AQI sensor unit and so far the air purifier sensor seems to correspond very well with it.Pros:- This unit is not super heavy and it is easy to move it around to different locations.- The filter unit can be cleaned with warm water so you don't have to constantly buy expensive HEPA filters every number of months.- The air purifier is quiet at lower settings.- The air purifier moves a lot of air through its filtration process allowing for quicker purification of a room when you need it to. I could literally feel a draft drawing in towards the air purifier when it is on the "turbo" setting.- There is a sleep mode so that if you are easily bothered by the lights at night, it can operate discreetly.Cons:- The panel is a bit difficult to remove when you need to access the filter and clean the sensor. There are two panels that look the same and it was hard to distinguish which panel should be removed in the manual. I wish this was made a bit more clear with a better mechanism to remove the panel since this air purifier will need regular cleaning and maintenance.- At "Turbo" mode, the air purifier is pretty loud but I feel like I would need to set it in this mode in very rare occasions.So far this air purifier is working well and I would highly recommend it if you're looking to clear the room of second hand smoke smell from neighbors. It is important that you also seal any and all gaps that the cigarette smell can enter your space. This air purifier will not magically "clean" the air if someone is actively smoking in the room. It will definitely help to get rid of pollutants from second or third-hand smoke so that you are breathing cleaner air.

body: Bought it at Costco a few days ago and absolutely love it. It really does make a difference in air quality.I absolutely love the Sleep mode, turns the fans to low and disables all lights, but still runs. And it's fairly quiet. It can get loud if you have it on turbo mode. But most of the time you won't.It's a simple and sleek design that will fit with most bright living rooms or bedrooms. And you can turn off the LED if you don't like it. The build quality is nice. It doesn't feel like cheap garbage plastic, and doesn't creak when applying pressure.Personally, I don't use the timer. Instead I wish it was replaced with a schedule. So I could set it to turn on when I'm at work or sleeping, and turn off when home. Though, for the price point, it's not a big deal.Replacement filters are easy to find despite what people say. You can find them on Amazon for $40 or on Cuckoo's own website under "Replacement Parts" for $50.So if you want an air filter for a small to medium sized room, and don't want to spend a Fortune, look no further.

body: Cleaning and set up was easy and it looks very nice. Everything is simple to use.
